questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements are correct with regard to the Satavahanas?
I. 	 Gautamiputra Satakarni was the founder of
the dynasty.
II. 	 During the Mauryan age they were part of the
Mauryan empire.
III. 	Their kingdom encompassed parts of present-
day Madhya Pradesh and Karnataka.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
In the Deccan, the Satavahanas established their independent rule after the decline of the Mauryas. Their rule lasted for about 450 years. They were also known as the Andhras.
● The **founder of this dynasty is known as Simuka** and he ruled from 235 BCE to 213 BCE. During the Mauryan age, **Satavahanas were part of the Mauryan Empire** but it appears that immediately after the fall of the dynasty, the Andhras declared themselves free. The third ruler of this dynasty, Satakarni I performed two Ashvamedhayajna. On the strength of his extensive military conquest, he has been referred as Lord of Dakshinapath. The next powerful ruler of this dynasty was Gautamiputra Satakarni whose achievements are recorded in glowing terms in the Nasik inscription. **So, statement I is not correct and** **statement II is correct.**
● Their kingdom also extended to parts of modern **Madhya Pradesh**, Gujarat, and **Karnataka**. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Satavahana** rulers were identified through **metronymics** (names derived from that of the mother).
● The succession to the throne among the Satavahanas was generally patrilineal. Some of the Satavahana rulers were **polygynous** (that is, had more than one wife). An examination of the names of women who married Satavahana rulers indicates that many of them had names derived from gotras such as Gotama and Vasistha, their **father’s** **gotras**.
○ They evidently retained these names instead of adopting names derived from their husband’s gotra name as they were required to do according to the Brahmanical rules. ○ What is also apparent is that some of these women belonged to the same gotra. As is obvious, this ran counter to the ideal of exogamy recommended in the Brahmanical texts. In fact, it exemplified an alternative practice, that of **gotra** **endogamy or marriage within the kin group**, which was (and is) prevalent amongst several communities in south India. Such marriages amongst kinfolk (such as cousins) ensured a close-knit community.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Dynasty Region Capital
I. - Satavahana Maharashtra Sisupalgarh
II. - Ikshvaku Andhra Pradesh
Vijayapuri
III. Chedi Odisha Pratishthana In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Satavahanas:** The most important native successors of the Mauryans in the Deccan and Central India were the Satavahanas. The later text Puranas use the name “Andhra” for the Satavahanas. Their capital was **Pratishtana** presently Paithan in Chhatrapati Sambhaji Nagar district (Maharashtra). Gradually, Satavahanas extended their power over Karnataka and Andhra. **So, row I is not correctly matched.**
● **Ikshvaku:** The Ikshvaku dynasty ruled in India’s Eastern Krishna river valley from their capital at **Vijayapuri** (modern **Nagarjunakonda**, Andhra Pradesh). The dynasty’s founder Vasishthiputra Chamtamula rose to power after the decline of the Satavahana power. **So, row II is correctly matched.**
● **Chedi:** After Ashoka, the kingdom of Kalinga gained prominence under the Chedi dynasty. King Kharavela in Hathigumpa inscription regards himself as the descendant of Mahameghvahana, linking his roots to the prehistoric Chedi Empire. Their capital city was **Sisupalgarh** in present-day Odisha. **So, row** **III is not correctly matched.** **So, in only one of the above rows the given information is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText: Which of the following ancient Indian dynasties is prominently associated with the use of the 'Ujjain symbol' on its coinage?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ujjain Symbol:** It comprises four **small balls attached to** **four bars**. It sometimes has a crescent above the rings. This symbol is a **significant feature of the Satavahana** **coinage**. This symbol is named by Alexander Cunningham for their common occurrence with the local Ujjain coins which indicate the connection of the Satavahanas and the western Kshatrapas.
● The Satavahanas issued **coins with portraits of their** **rulers imprinted on them**. The coins were usually made of gold, silver, copper, bronze, lead, or an alloy of lead called Potin.  Prakrit, Telugu and Kannada dialects were used in some coins. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which of the following books were written by Harsha?
I. 	 Ratnavali
II. 	 Nagananda
III. 	Priyardarsika
IV. 	 Harshacharita
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The decline of the Gupta Empire was followed by a period of political disorder and disunity in North India. It was only in the beginning of the seventh century A.D. that Harshvardhana succeeded in establishing a larger kingdom in north India.
● The chief sources for tracing the history of Harsha and his times are the **Harshacharita written by Bana** and the Travel accounts of Hiuen Tsang. **Bana was the court poet of Harsha**. **So, point IV is not correct.**
○ Hiuen Tsang was the Chinese traveler who visited India in the seventh century A.D.
● Besides these two sources, the **dramas written by Harsha**, namely **Ratnavali**, **Nagananda** and **Priyardarsika** also provide useful information. **So, points I, II and III are correctly matched.**
● The Madhuben plate inscription and the Sonpat inscription are also helpful to know the chronology of Harsha’s reign. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Prayagraj prashasti (Allahabad Prashasti):
I. 	 It mentions the accession and conquests of
Skandagupta.
II. 	 It was composed by Harisena in Sanskrit
language and Brahmi script.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Prayagraj Prashasti** (till now known as the **Allahabad Prashasti**) in prose and verse, is a retrospective glowing account of **Samudragupta’s** personality, personal qualities, greatness and glorious exploits.
● It is the most important source which gives us information about the accession and conquests of **Samudragupta. So, statement I is not correct.**
○ **Bhitari pillar inscription is about Skandagupta,** who restored the Gupta power by defeating his enemies, who may have been rebels or foreign invaders.
■ At the end of his reign, most parts of north India were directly under the control of Samudragupta, **except Kashmir**, western Punjab, Rajasthan, Sindh and Gujarat.
● **Prayagraj Prashasti** was composed by his court poet **Harishena** who composed 33 lines which were engraved on a pillar. It is incised in **Sanskrit language and Brahmi** **script**. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs with regard to the Gupta empire:
Official Function
I. - Sandivigraha Minister of foreign affairs
II. - Prathama Kulika Chief craftsman
III. Kumaramatya Village headman
IV. Nagara Sreshti Chief banker of the city
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Guptas were the rulers of the Magadha (now Bihar) state in northeastern India. They maintained an empire over northern and parts of central and western India from the early 4th to the late 6th century CE. **Administration of the Guptas:**
● The Gupta kings assumed titles like Paramabhattaraka, Maharajadhiraja, Parameswara, Samrat and Chakravartin. The king was assisted in his administration by a council consisting of a chief minister, a Senapati or commander-in-chief of the army and other important officials.
● A high official called **Sandivigraha** was mentioned in the Gupta inscriptions, who most **probably was** **the minister for foreign affairs**. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● In the administrative structure, the **Nagara Sreshthi** was responsible for **overseeing urban governance** **and city administration**. At the rural level, **villages** within a district were **under the authority of** **Gramikas**, who managed local affairs. Among the artisan community, the **Prathama Kulika served as** **the chief artisan.** **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● The king maintained a close contact with the **provincial administration** through a class of officials called **Kumaramatyas and Ayuktas**. **Provinces** in the Gupta Empire were known as **Bhuktis and provincial** **governors** as **Uparikas**. They were mostly chosen from among the princes. **Bhuktis were subdivided** **into Vishyas** or districts. They were **governed by Vishyapatis**. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● In some kingdoms**, Nagara Sreshti** also served as the **chief banker or merchant** of the city. **So, pair IV** **is correctly matched.** **So, only three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which of the following Kings’ reign is noteworthy for the visit of the Chinese pilgrim Hiuen Tsang?
I. 	 Harshavardhana
II. 	 Narasimhavarman I
III. 	Mahendravarman I
IV. 	 Pulakesin II
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Hiuen Tsang was the Chinese traveler who visited India in the seventh century A.D. He visited the Kingdoms of various rulers and gave detailed accounts of their reign.
● **Harshavardhana** who belonged to the Pushyabhuti dynasty was **converted to Mahayana Buddhism by** **Hiuen Tsang**. He also organised a religious assembly at Kanauj to honour the Chinese pilgrim Hiuen Tsang towards the close of his reign. He invited representatives of all religious sects. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Narasimhavarman I (630-668 A.D.)** of the Pallava dynasty was also known as Mamalla, which means ‘great wrestler’. During his reign, **Hiuen Tsang visited the Pallava capital Kanchipuram**. His description of Kanchi is vivid. He calls it a big and beautiful city, six miles in circumference. It had 100 Buddhist monasteries in which about 10,000 Buddhist monks lived. According to his account the people of Kanchi esteemed great learning and the Ghatika at Kanchi served as a great centre of learning. Narasimhavarman I was the founder of Mamallapuram and the monolithic rathas were erected during his reign. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Pulakesin II (608-642 A.D.)** was the most important **ruler of the Chalukyan dynasty**. The Aihole inscription issued by him gives the details of his reign. The most important event in the reign of Pulakesin II was the **visit of Hiuen Tsang** to his kingdom. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **Mahendravarman I** who belonged to the Pallava dynasty was not visited by Hiuen Tsang. **So, point III** **is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText: Vikram Samvat, beginning in the 57 BC, is traditionally associated with:

options.a:
Vikramaditya, commemorating his victory
over the Sakas
options.b: Ashoka, to mark his conquest of Kalinga
options.c:
Kanishka, to commemorate the establishment
of Kushana rule
options.d:
Pushyamitra Shunga, to mark the foundation
of the Shunga dynasty

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The term ‘Saka’ is used for the people called the **Scythians**, who originally belong to Central Asia. The Bactrian Greeks were attacked by several nomadic tribes from Central Asia, which led to the downfall of the Greek kingdom in India.  The most important of these tribes were the Sakas, who eventually established their control over large territories. **Mathura, Ujjain and Girnar** were centers of Saka rulers in north India.
● Maues, the first Saka ruler in India, established Saka power in the region of Gandhara. **Later, a ruler of** **Ujjain defeated the Sakas, assumed the title Vikramaditya, and commemorated his victory by** **initiating the Vikram Samvat era in 57 BCE.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
King Title
I. - Gautamiputra Satakarni
Ekabrahmana
II. - Chandragupta II Thrisamudratoya Peethavahana
III. - Harsha Mahameghavahana
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The greatest ruler of the Satavahana dynasty was Gautamiputra Satakarni**, who reigned from 106 to 130 CE for a period of 24 years. His achievements are recorded in the Nasik inscription, issued by his mother, Gautami Balasri, also known as the Queen Mother Edict. **He assumed the title Ekabrahmana** and is credited with significant military and administrative accomplishments**. So, pair I is correctly** **matched.**
○ The **Nasik prashasti** states that the horses of **Gautamiputra** drank waters of the three oceans”(the Arabian Sea, the Bay of Bengal and the Indian Ocean) which gave him the title of **Thrisamudratoya Peethavahana.** **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● The kingdom of Kalinga gained prominence under the Chedi dynasty. **King Kharavela** (ruled approximately around the first century BCE) in Hathigumpa inscription regards himself as the descendant of **Mahameghvahana**, linking his roots to the prehistoric Chedi Empire. The kings of this dynasty claimed the title of **Mahameghvahana. So,pair III is not correctly matched.** **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the administration during Pallava period:
I. 	 The Ur was an assembly of brahmin
landowners.
II. 	 The Nagara was an assembly of mercantile
groups.
III. 	The variyams supervised the civic and temple
affairs.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Pallavas** had a well organized administrative system. **The Pallava state was divided into Kottams.** The Kottam was administered by officers appointed by the king. The king was at the centre of administration in which he was assisted by able ministers. He was the fountain of justice.
● The inscriptions of the Pallavas mention a number of local assemblies: ○ These included the **sabha, which was an assembly of brahmin land owners**. This assembly functioned through subcommittees, which looked after irrigation, agricultural operations, making roads, local temples, etc.
■ The **ur was a village assembly found in areas where the land owners were not** **brahmins**. **So, statement I is not correct.** ■ And the **nagaram was an organisation of merchants.** It is likely that these assemblies were controlled by rich and powerful landowners and merchants. Many of these local assemblies continued to function for centuries. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Variyam or committee system** became a **hallmark of self-government in the Brahman settlements.** The **Sabha through its Variyam system supervised** the **maintenance of roads and tanks, management** **of charitable donations, regulation of irrigational rights** and **temple affairs**. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 A well organised spy system in the empire
II. 	 Kapilavastu as a major trading center
III. 	Absence of death penalty in Magadha
Which of the above was/were observed by Fahien, on his visit to India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● During the reign of Chandragupta II (Vikramaditya), the Buddhist monk **Fahien** visited India. He was the first Chinese traveller to India. His travel accounts provided information about the socio-economic, religious and moral conditions of the people of the Gupta age.
● **Fahien’s account on the Gupta administration** provides useful information. ○ He characterises the Gupta administration as mild and benevolent. There were **no restrictions on** **people’s movements** and they enjoyed a large degree of personal freedom. ○ There was no state interference in the individual’s life. Punishments were not severe. Imposing a fine was a common punishment. **There was no spy system**. The administration was so efficient that the roads were kept safe for travelers, and there was no fear of thieves. **So, point I is not** **correct.**
○ He mentioned that **people were generally prosperous and the crimes were negligible**. Fahien had also appreciated the efficiency of the Gupta administration as he was able to travel without any fear throughout the Gangetic valley. On the whole the administration was more liberal than that of the Mauryas. According to Fahien, **the people of Magadha were happy and prosperous**, that justice was mildly administered and **there was no death penalty**. **So, point III is correct.**
● Gaya was desolated. **Kapilavasthu had become a jungle**, but at **Pataliputra people were rich and** **prosperous**. **So, point II is not correct.**
● In essence, Fa-Hien's account paints a picture of a prosperous, peaceful, and religiously diverse India with a strong emphasis on morality and charity. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
In the context of Buddhist philosophy, which
one of the following correct defines the term “Magga”?

options.b: The truth of the origin of suffering
options.c: The truth of the cessation of suffering
options.d:
The truth of the path to the cessation of
suffering

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The following are **Four Noble Truths:** ○ The truth of suffering (**Dukkha**) ○ The truth of the origin of suffering (**Samudāya**) ○ The truth of the cessation of suffering (**Nirodha**) ○ The truth of the path to the cessation of suffering (**Magga**)
● **Right action and Right speech are among the eightfold path taught by Buddha**. Buddha did not involve himself in the fruitless controversies regarding the soul (atman) and the Brahma which raged strongly during his time. He addressed himself to worldly problems. He said that the world is full of sorrow and people suffer on account of desires. If desires are conquered, nirvana will be attained, that is man will be free from the cycle of birth and death. Unlike Hinduism, it **does not believe there is an** **eternal, unchanging “soul” that transmigrates from one life to the next**. Buddha neither accepted nor rejected the existence of God. **Buddhism does not recognize the existence of God and soul.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Regulation of trade practices
II. 	 Quality control of goods
III. 	Providing loans to the traders
IV. 	 Training new artisans
How many of the above functions were performed by the Shrenis during the Gupta Period?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In ancient India, **Sreni or guilds** played a significant role in shaping the economic and social fabric of society. The guilds were associations of people engaged in the same craft or profession and were prevalent in various parts of India from around 600 BCE to 1200 CE, including the Gupta Period.
● These guilds functioned as powerful socio-economic institutions that **regulated trade, maintained** **quality standards,** and protected the interests of craftsmen. **So, points I and II are correct.**
● They acted as cooperative associations where artisans of similar trades—such as blacksmiths, weavers, goldsmiths, carpenters, potters, and jewelers—worked collectively.
● Guilds also served as financial entities, **providing loans and acting as banks** by issuing their own coins. **So, point III is correct.**
● These guilds also played a role in **training new artisans**, ensuring that skills were passed down through generations. **So, point IV is correct.**
● They enjoyed considerable **autonomy** and sometimes even **held administrative responsibilities** within their localities.
● The state often recognized guilds officially, granting them tax exemptions or land to support their economic functions. Some artisans worked independently, while others were employed by guilds or even directly by the state for large-scale construction projects, such as temples and palaces. **So, all four of the above functions were performed by the Shrenis during the Gupta Period.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct
with regard to the administration of the Satvahana dynasty?
I. 	 The Gaulmika was a civic body that looked
after the town administration.
II. 	 The Nigama sabha was primarily responsible
for village administration.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In the Satavahana kingdom, the king was the highest official whose position was hereditary.  They retained some of the administrative features of the  Mauryas.  The district was called Ahara and the officials were known as Amatyas and Mahamatras. The royal princes (kumaras) were appointed as viceroys of the provinces. The **administration in the rural areas** was placed in the hands of **Gaulmika**.  **So, statement** **I is not correct.**
● The Satavahana kingdom mainly had three types of settlements: **nagara** (city), **nigama** (market town) and **gama** (village).  Each town was administered by a civic body known as **‘Nigama Sabha’**, which had a representative character. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
In the context of history of ancient India, the terms
‘Kshetra’, ‘Khila’ and ‘Aprahata’ refer to:

options.a: Tax exempted villages in the Mauryan empire
options.b:
Types of land grants during the Satavahana
period
options.c: Land divisions in the Gupta era

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Gupta Empire, from the 4th to the 6th century CE, marked a classical age in North India, renowned for its extensive territory and cultural zenith.
● The concern of the Gupta society with agricultural production is clear from the way various aspects associated with agricultural operations are mentioned in the sources of the Gupta period.
● The **various types of land** classified during Gupta period, as mentioned in the inscriptions are: ○ **Kshetra** - cultivable land ○ **Khila** - waste land ○ **Aprahata** - jungle or forest land ○ **Vasti** - habitable land ○ **Gapata Saraha** - pastoral land
● Inscriptions give the impression that **uncultivated land was being regularly brought** under cultivation.
● **Classification of land** according to **soil, fertility and the use** to which it was put was not unknown. Different land measures were known in different regions, although one cannot be certain what exact measure was denoted by a term. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● In some areas of the Gupta Empire, **Nivartana** was the term used for a measure of land whereas in the inscriptions of Bengal terms like **Kulyavapa** and **Dronavapa** are used.
● It is not possible to classify the regions precisely according to the crops grown, but all the major categories of crops--**cereals like barley, wheat and paddy, different varieties of** **pulses, grams and vegetables as well as cash crops like cotton and sugarcane** were known long before the Gupta period and continued to be cultivated.
● However, crops like **maize or vegetables like potatoes or tomatoes** were not known to the farmers of the Gupta period.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct
with regard to the Sangam society?
I. 	 Virakkal refers to the practice of hero-stone
worship, wherein memorial stones were erected to honor soldiers who had died heroically in battle.
II. 	 Kadimaram was a sacred river associated with
royal ceremonial rituals, often performed by kings prior to embarking on military campaigns.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Warfare was a major occupation of the ruling classes of the** **Sangam period.** Generally war started with the well-known incident of **cattle-fighting**. The incident was both a protest and a justification. There was no dearth of wars in the Sangam age. The kings maintained armies and weapons necessary for it. The art of warfare was fairly advanced.
● The **institution of** **virakkal** **or** **naḍukal** (hero-stone) was a **practice of erecting memorial stones for martyred** **soldiers**, honoring and worshipping them in remembrance of the bravery they displayed in battle. Many hero stones with legends inscribed on them were found in different parts of Tamil Nadu. **So, statement I** **is correct.**
● Each ruler of the Sangam Age maintained a regular army and had a **Kodimaram** **or** **Kadimaram** **(tutelary** **tree)** as a symbol of authority. A sacred tree associated with this tradition was typically installed within the palace premises, signifying royal power. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Religious Scholar of Medieval India
Associated With
I. - Jangama Vajrayana tradition
II. - Mahasiddhas Lingayat tradition
III. - Ulema Islamic Sharia
IV. - Gurukkal Shaiva tradition
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Medieval India** saw the rise of influential religious scholars and reformers who shaped the religious and philosophical landscape.
● **Jangama** refers to a specific type of wandering religious teacher or mendicant within the **Lingayat/Veerashaiva tradition**. Jangamas are considered spiritual guides and are equated with gurus and even the divine Linga. They are known for their nomadic lifestyle, selfless service, and imparting spiritual and religious knowledge. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● In **Vajrayana Buddhism,** the equivalent of a wandering spiritual teacher or ascetic is often referred to as **Mahasiddhas**. These individuals are revered as highly accomplished practitioners of tantric Buddhism, known for their mastery of spiritual practices and often unconventional lifestyles. Flourishing between the 8th and 12th centuries in India, particularly in eastern regions like Bengal, Bihar, and the Himalayan foothills, they were central figures in the rise of Vajrayana and Tantric Buddhism. **So, pair II is not** **correctly matched.**
● **Ulema** (plural of alim, or one who knows) are **scholars of Islamic studies**. As preservers of this tradition they perform various religious, juridical and teaching functions.  Ulema played an important role in the politics of medieval India. They attracted the creative social, religious and intellectual energies in the country to become instrumental for social and cultural change. The Ulema were that part of the Muslim Community learned in literature, law and doctrines of Islam. They were judges, jurists, prayers, leaders, scholars, teachers, readers of Quran, recitals of traditions, Sufis, functionaries of mosques and so on. Theoretically, Muslim rulers were to be guided by the ulema, who were expected to ensure that they ruled according to the **shari‘a**. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Gurukkal** were **temple priests and ritual specialists** in **Shaiva Hinduism**, primarily found in Tamil Nadu and parts of South India. Gurukkals traditionally belong to the Adi Shaiva or Shaiva Vellalar communities and have served as custodians of temple worship since at least the Chola period (9th–13th
centuries CE). Their main function is to perform Agamic rituals, daily pujas, and other temple ceremonies dedicated to Lord Shiva. While mainly associated with Tamil Shaivism, the term is also used in Kerala, where it can refer to **masters of Kalaripayattu** (martial arts), indicating a broader connotation of teacher or spiritual guide. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
In the context of ancient history of India, consider
the following statements:
I. 	 Satavahanas were the first native Indians to
issue coins with portraits of their kings.
II. 	 Guptas started the practice of giving royal
grants of land to Brahmanas and Buddhist monks.
III. 	Pushyabhutis were the first to take significant
steps to regulate the practice of Sati.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Satavahanas were the first native Indians who had issued the coins with portraits of their kings**. The coins issued by them were usually made of gold, silver, copper, bronze, lead, or an alloy of lead called Potin. Prakrit dialect was used in inscriptions of coins. They issued the silver coin called **Karshapannas.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Satavahanas** are also known in history for starting the practice of giving **royal grants of land to** **Brahmanas and Buddhist monks**, including those associated with tax exemptions. **So, statement II is** **not correct.**
● During the period of **Harsha of the Pushybhuti dynasty**, the position of women was not satisfactory. The institution of Swyamvara (the choice of choosing her husband) had declined. Remarriage of widows was not permitted, particularly among the higher castes. The system of dowry had also become common. **The practice of sati was also prevalent**. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Mathematician Contribution
I. - Brahmagupta Value of ‘pi’
II. - Varahamihira Siddhantic Astronomy
III. Bhaskara II Siddhanta Shiromani
IV - Vararuchi Katapayadi system
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Golden (Siddhantic) period of Indian mathematics begins with Aryabhata I**, who was born in A.D. 496, a pioneer mathematician known for his systematic collection and systematisation of knowledge, and ends with Bhaskara II, born in A.D. 1114 who put the knowledge of mathematics on a solid foundation. The mathematicians in between them -Varahamihira (A.D. 505), Bhaskara I (A.D. 600), Brahmagupta (A.D. 628), Mahavira (A.D. 850), Sridhara (A.D. 850), Sripati (A.D. 1039), were equally famous.
● The **first compilation of mathematical results**, in the context of the **construction of fire altars,** is found in the **Shrautasutra of Baudhayana,** who was born around 800 BCE. However, he implies that he is presenting knowledge passed down through tradition. The **Baudhayana Sulbasutra** is noted for containing several early mathematical results, including an **approximation of the square root of 2 and** **a statement of the Pythagorean theorem.** **Baudhayana** is considered **one of the first to approximate** **the value of pi,** which is mentioned in his Sulbasutras. According to his text, the approximate value of pi is 3**. So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
○ **Brahmagupta** has two sections in his **Brahmasphuṭasiddhanta** —Gaṇita (mathematics) and Kuṭṭaka (pulveriser). Brahmasphuṭasiddhānta covers a wide range of topics including **arithmetic,** **algebra, geometry, trigonometry, and astronomy.**
● **Aryabhatiya**, the influential 5th century treatise on Mathematics and Astronomy, included Aryabhata’s innovations like Trigonometry as well as knowledge passed down. **Aryabhatta worked out the accurate**
**value of π to 3.1416** in 499AD. He introduced **Siddhantic Astronomy using his Trigonometry. So, pair** **II is not correctly matched.**
○ The major works of **Varahamihira** include the **Pancha-Siddhantika**, a treatise on mathematical astronomy summarizing five earlier astronomical systems, and the **Brihat Samhita,** a comprehensive encyclopedia covering topics like astronomy, astrology, geography, etc.
● It is believed that **Vararuchi**, who lived during the Gupta period, introduced the **Katapayadi system** of representing ten based numbers using Sanskrit alphabets, in his work **Chandravakyas.** The place value of the number probably became explicit from this time. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.**
● **Bhaskara I** was an Indian mathematician and astronomer of the 7th century. He is known for his **commentary on the Aryabhatiya** by Aryabhata. His work on astronomy was influenced by Aryabhata's system.
● **Bhaskara II** is renowned for his works **Siddhanta Shiromani**, **Lilavati, and Bijaganita,** which show how the knowledge of mathematics has expanded in volumes in this period. **So, pair III is correctly** **matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Baudhayana:**
● The Baudhayana sutras consist of six texts: ○ Srautasutra, ○ Karmantasutra , ○ Dwaidhasutra, ○ Grihyasutra, ○ Dharmasutra and ○ Sulbasutra. **More on Aryabhata:**
● Aryabhaṭiya has two sections—Dasagtika (some essential parameters on decimal scale and discovery of zero, elements of trigonometry) and Gaṇita (eight fundamental operations, plane geometry, algebraical equations and their solutions).
● The second chapter of Aryabhatta’s work, Ganitapada, exclusively deals with Mathematics, where Arithmetics, Algebra, Trigonometry and some results in geometry are systematically introduced.’ Zero’ became an integral part of the system.
● Aryabhata used a ten based number system with up to 10 digits to work with large numbers. His Ardha-jya and Kona-jya are our sine and cosine functions..

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the land grants given during the Gupta period:
I. 	 The Agrahara were hereditary grants given to
the Brahmanas along with the assignment of
all land revenue.
II. 	 The Devagrahara grants were tax-free lands
given to military officials in exchange for
their service.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The sources of the Gupta period suggest that certain important changes were taking place in the agrarian society. **Feudal development** surfaced under the Guptas with the grant of fiscal and administrative concessions to priests and administrators. Started in the Deccan by the Satavahanas, the practice became a regular affair in Gupta times.
● Religious functionaries were granted **land, free of tax, forever** and they were authorised to collect from the peasants all the taxes which could have otherwise gone to the emperor.
● Religious grants were of two types: ○ The **Agrahara grants** were meant for the **Brahmanas** which meant to be perpetual, hereditary and tax-free, accompanied with the assignment of all land revenue. **So, statement I is correct.** ○ **The Devagrahara grants were given to secular parties such as writers and merchants for the** **repair and worship of temples.** Such secular donations are evident in a grant made by the Uccakalpa dynasty. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Two villages were bestowed as a mark of favour, in perpetuity with fiscal and administrative rights upon a person called **Pulindabhatta**. Epigraphic evidence of land grants made to officers for the administrative and military services is lacking, though such grants cannot be ruled out.
● In fact, certain designations of administrative officers such as **bhagika and bhogapalika** suggest that some of the state officials may have been remunerated by land grants. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are the major epics of the
Sangam age?
I. 	 Kundalakesi
II. 	 Silappadikaram
III. 	Manimekhlai
IV. 	 Valayapathi
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The word ‘**Sangam’** refers to the **association** **of poets** who flourished under the royal patronage of the Pandya kings at Madurai. The poems composed by these poets are collectively known as Sangam literature. The period in which these poems were composed is called the Sangam Age. **Muvendar** (meaning "three chiefs" or "three kings) – the Cheras, Cholas and the Pandyas–controlled the territories of Tamizhagam during the Sangam Age.
● **Silappadikaram** and **Manimekhlai** are regarded as the two major Tamil epics linked to the post-Sangam literary tradition. **So, points II and** **III are correct.**
○ **Silappadikaram**, authored by **Ilango Adigal**, narrates the story of Kovalan and Kannagi. It also provides insight into Tamil society, polity, and social life. It is often called the ‘Iliad of Tamil Literature’. ○ **Manimekhlai**, authored by **Seethalai Sattanar**, is the sequel to Silappadikaram, narrating the journey of Manimekhlai, daughter of Kovalan and Madhavi, who becomes a Buddhist monk. The epic explores Sangam society, philosophy, and Buddhism in South India, and is often referred to as the ‘Odissi of Tamil literature’.
● The **other major Sangam epics** include **Tholkappiyam, Ettuthogai** (eight anthologies), **Pathupattu** (ten idylls), **PathinanKeezhkanakku** (a collection of eighteen poetic works), **Pattinapalai** and **Maduraikanji.**
● **Kundalakesi and Valayapathi** are **not classified** among the major Sangam epics. They belong to a later phase of Tamil literary development. **So, point I and IV are not correct.**
○ **Valayapathi**, authored by a **Tamil Jain monk**, narrates the story of a son seeking his absent father. It supported the ideologies found in early **Jainism.**
○ **Kundalakesi**, authored by **Nathakuthanaar**, tells a story about love, marriage, getting tired of the married partner, murder and then discovering religion**.** It is related to **Buddhism.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● According to Tamil scholars, **three sangams** (academies of Tamil poets) existed in ancient Tamil Nadu, also known as **Muchchangam**. Under the **Pandyan dynasty’s** royal patronage, these Sangam flourished. These Tamil literary works continue to be valuable resources for rewriting Sangam age history.
○ **First Sangam: Legendary gods and sages** attended the first Sangam held in **Madurai**, although there are no recorded accounts of it. ○ **Second Sangam:** It was held in **Kapadapuram** in Tamilnadu.Except for **Tolkappiyam**, none of the literary works written during this Sangam survived. ○ **Third Sangam:** The third Sangam was established in **Madurai by** **Mudathirumaran**. Although many poets participated and created substantial work, only some persisted. ○ **Pathinen Melkanakku** includes **Ettutogai and Pattuppattu**.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the history of south India:
I. 	 Korkai was a renowned port for pearl trading
under the Pandya dynasty.
II. 	 Kallanai dam across the Kaveri River was
constructed by the Cholas.
III. 	Muziris emerged as a key trading center with
the Roman Empire under the Cheras.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
During the Sangam Age, the Tamil region was ruled by three dynasties, namely the **Chera, Chola and Pandyas.** They were together, also known as **Muvendar**.
● **Korkai:** Since the beginning of the first millennium B.C., Korkai is **the oldest port site of the Tamil** **region**. However, its emergence as a noteworthy emporium may have been only around the Fourth and Fifth Century B.C. Korkai is also called **Madurodayanallur and Madurantakanallur**.
○ Korkai was the center **of pearl trade** and produced pearls that were sought after not only in Tamilakam, but in the kingdoms of north India and Rome. The **Pandyan dynasty** primarily ruled their empire from Korkai, a sea port on the southernmost tips of the Indian Peninsula, and in later times moved to Madurai. **So, statement I is correct.** ○ The first Pandyan **king Nedunj Cheliyan I,** who ruled from the coastal town of Korkai, at the mouth of river **Tamirabarani**, has been mentioned in the Sangam works.
● **Kallanai Dam:** It is also called **Grand Anaicut.** It is situated in the center of the districts of Thanjavur and Trichy in **Tamil Nadu**.
○ This dam, which was constructed by **Karikalan Cholan of the Chola Dynasty** about 1,900 years ago, is the **oldest still-existing water-diversion construction** in India and the **fourth-oldest in** **the world.** ○ The dam is situated on the **Kaveri River.** Its main objective was to use canals to redirect waterways from the Kaveri River for agriculture across the rich delta region and to Kollidam/Coleroon, the northern delta branch. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Muziris:** It was an **ancient harbour and urban centre** on India's **Malabar Coast**. ○ It was a major trading center that facilitated significant trade between the **Chera kingdom and** **the Roman Empire**, especially during the early centuries CE. ○ This ancient port, located in present day **Kerala**, was a thriving hub for the **exchange of goods** **like black pepper** and other spices for Roman commodities. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following administrative
divisions of the ancient Tamil kingdoms is correctly arranged from smallest to largest?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Ancient Tamil kingdoms, during the **Sangam age**, had a well-organized system for dividing land, based on population, economy, and location. The kingdom was organized into different administrative divisions to ensure effective local governance.
● The entire kingdom was called **Mandalam**.
● Mandalam was divided into **Nadus**. **Kurram** was a subdivision of Nadu.
● The **Ur** was a village, classified into **perur** (big village), **Sirur** (a small village) and **Mudur** (an old village) depending upon its population, size and antiquity.
● **Pattinam** was the name for a coastal town and **Puhar** was the general term for harbour town. **So, the correct sequence, in terms of smallest to** **largest, is: Sirur – Kurram – Nadu – Mandalam.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the sources of revenue in the Sangam Age:
I. 	 Kudimai was paid by the cultivating tenants
to the landlords.
II. 	 Karai was a tax imposed on salt production,
which was under the state's royal monopoly.
III. 	Variyam was a tax yielding territory headed
by an officer called a Variyar.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In the Sangam age, **various kinds of taxes** were known and collected. Out of that revenue, the king incurred his private and public expenditures.
● A **number of officials** were appointed by the king to look after the revenue affairs. The king levied the various taxes according to the customs. Unjust and unusual taxation, the demand for forced gifts, etc. were not approved.
● Like other functionaries of the state, the surveyors of the land called **naduvagai seykira** too hailed from the landholding communities. Various units of the land measurement such as **kuli, ma, veli, patti,** **padagam, etc.** are known, with local variations. Generally, taxes were collected in different forms. The taxes collected included **irai, kanikadan, iraikattina-kanikadan and kadamai.**
● An important category of tax was **kudimai.** Kudimai was paid by the cultivating tenants to the government and to the landlords, the **bearers of honorific titles such as udaiyan, araiyan and kilavar. So, statement** **I is correct.**
● Among the revenue of the state, **land revenue** was the chief source of income. The **land tax** was called **irai or karai**, but the share of the agricultural produce, claimed and collected by the king, is not specified. The land tax was paid in **cash or kind**. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● A well-known **unit of territory yielding tax** was a **variyam (the term “vari” meant tax)** and an officer in-charge of collecting the tax from that unit of land was called a **Variyar.** The **Pattinappalai** refers to the custom officials employed in the seaport of Puhar. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **What were the other sources of revenue in the Sagam age?**
● The tax rates were fixed depending on the fertility of the soil and the status of the landholder. **Opati were levied and collected by the king and local chiefs**. Temples and Brahmins were exempted from paying the taxes.
● The **tax paid in kind was referred to as iraikattina-nellu**. All these were mostly realised from the Cauvery delta but not widely in the outskirts of the kingdom.
● The **ma and veli** was the measure of land and **kalam** as the measure of grain. It was considered to be the legitimate share of the king.
● The **tolls and customs duties**, which were called **ulgu or sungam,** were levied at all the **seaports**. Mainly the tolls were collected on the **trunk roads and at the** **frontiers** of the kingdom.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs with regard to the
Chandra Gupta II:
Navaratna Work
I. - Harisena Poet
II. - Sanku Architect
III. - Amarasimha Astronomer
IV - Kahapanaka Astrologer
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Chandragupta II (375-415 AD) (Vikramaditya)** is said to have assembled the greatest writers and artists called Navaratna (Nine Jewels) in his court. Kalidasa is said to be one among them.
● **Navaratna in the court of Vikramaditya:** ○ Kalidasa: Sanskrit poet ○ **Harisena: Sanskrit poet. So, pair I is correctly matched.** ○ **Sanku: Architect. So, pair II is correctly matched.** ○ **Amarasimha: Lexicographer. So, pair III is not correctly matched.** ○ Dhanvantri: Physician ○ **Kahapanaka: Astrologer. So, pair IV is correctly matched.** ○ Varahamihira: Astronomer ○ Varauchi: Grammarian and Sanskrit scholar ○ Vittalbhatta: Magician **So, only three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
In which of the following languages the Pallavas
issued their charters?
I. 	 Sanskrit
II. 	 Tamil
III. 	Prakrit
IV. 	 Pali
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Pallavas** established their kingdom in **Tondaimandalam** with its capital at **Kanchipuram**. The early Pallava rulers from 250 A.D. to 350 A.D. Their rule continued till Tondaimandalam was captured and annexed by the Imperial Cholas in the beginning of the tenth century A.D.
● They issued their charters in **Prakrit**. Important among them were Sivaskandavarman and Vijayaskandavarman. **So, point III is correct.**
● The second line of Pallava rulers who ruled between 350 A.D. and 550 A.D. issued their charters in **Sanskrit**. **So, point I is correct.**
○ The most important ruler of this line was **Vishnugopa** who was defeated by Samudragupta during his South Indian expedition.
● The rulers of the third line who ruled from 575 A.D. to their ultimate fall in the ninth century issued their charters both in **Sanskrit and Tamil**. **So, point II is correct.**
○ **Simhavishnu** was the first ruler of this line. He destroyed the Kalabhras and firmly established the Pallava rule in Tondaimandalam. He also defeated the Cholas and extended the Pallava territory up to the river Kaveri. ○ Other great Pallava rulers of this line were **Mahendravarman I, Narasimhavarman I, and** **Narasimhavarman II.**
● Pali was mainly used in **Buddhist contexts;** it was not a language in which Pallava charters were issued. **So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
Which of the following titles were associated with
Mahendravarman I of the Pallava dynasty?
I. 	 Chettakari
II. 	 Chitrakarapuli
III. 	Vichitrachitta
IV.	 Mattavilasa
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Mahendravarman I (590-630 CE), of the Pallava dynasty** succeeded his father Simhavishnu. Under him the Pallavas became a major political power. He was a king with many accomplishments.
● He is known for his **literary works and temple building activities** and he was also a **skilled veena** **player.** He promoted many artistic activities like drama, painting, and music.
● He assumed a number of titles: ○ **Mattavilasa** (addicted to pleasures). **So, point IV is correct.** ○ **Chitrakarapuli** (tiger among the painters). **So, point II is correct.** ○ **Lalitankura** (charming offspring) ○ **Gunabhara** (virtuous) ○ **Chettakari** (temple builder). **So, point I is correct.** ○ **Vichitrachitta** (curious-minded). **So, point III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● Mahendravarman I was a **follower of Jainism but converted to Saivism** under the influence of Tirunavukkarasu or Appar. He studied music under Rudracharya and composed exercises for the practice of students on a variety of Vina known as Parivadini. He has authored the Sanskrit work **“Mastavilasa Prahasanam‟.**
● He was a great builder of cave temples. The **rock cut caves at Mandagapattu,** Dalavanur and Tiruchirapalli were excavated during his time.
● The **Jain paintings found in the rock cut caves at Sittannavasal** located in the present Pudukkottai region are attributed to him. He built a Siva temple at Tiruvadi.
● The long drawn **Pallava-Chalukya conflict** began during this period. Mahendravarman I **was defeated by Pulakesin II at a place called Pullalur near** **Kanchi**. Pulakesin II almost reached the Pallava capital, but Mahendravarman purchased peace by ceding their Northern provinces to the Pulakesin II.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
In the context architecture of southern India, the
place of ‘Kudimiyanmalai’ is famous for:

options.a:
Inscriptions having carvings of bharatanatyam
dance postures
options.b:
Hosting the only monolithic lingam in South
India

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **c**
**Explanation:**
● **Kudimiyanmalai in Tamil Nadu**, is renowned for its rock-cut cave temple dedicated to Lord Shiva. The **shiva temple** is located on a hill lock dedicated to **Sikkanatheeswarer** and life size sculptures were surrounded by this temple.
● There are more than 100 inscriptions with a highlight of an inscription expressing the **grammatical notes** **of** **Carnatic music.**
● The temple is also noted for the rock-cut panel depicting **sixty-three nayanmar (saints)** worshipping Siva and Parvati.
● **Jain vestiges** in the form of Tamil-Brahmi inscription found on the south-western slope of the hill proves the timeline of the temple to many centuries back.
● The famous musical inscription describing **notations for** **the seven-stringed musical instrument ‘Parivadini’** is engraved to the right of the cave temple. The **mahamandapa** attached with the structural temple has exquisite pillars and portrait sculptures of the Nayaka period. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 He built the Shore temple at Mamallapuram
and the Kailasanatha temple at Kanchipuram.
II. 	 His court was adorned by the famous Sanskrit
scholar Dandin.
III. 	He sent embassies to China.
IV. 	 He assumed titles like Sankarabhakta,
Vadhyavidyadhara and Agamapriya. Who was this ruler?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Narasimhavarman II or Rajasimha (695 -722 A.D.)** became the ruler of the Pallava kingdom, after succeeding Mahendravarman II and Parameswarvarman I.
● His regime was peaceful and he evinced more interest in developing art and architecture. The Shore temple at **Mamallapuram and the Kailasanatha temple at Kanchipuram** were built in this period. He was also a great patron of art and letters.
● The famous Sanskrit scholar **Dandin** is said to have adorned his court. He sent embassies to **China** and the maritime trade flourished during his reign. He assumed titles like **Sankarabhakta,** **Vadhyavidyadhara and Agamapriya.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct
with regard to the Pallava era?
I. 	 It witnessed the rise of Saivism and
Vaishnavism and the subsequent decline of
Buddhism and Jainism.
II. 	 The Ghatika at Kanchi was a popular centre
of learning.
III. 	The Dravidian style of temple architecture
began during this period.
IV. 	 The first stone and mortar temples of South
India were constructed during this period.
How many of the statements given above are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Pallavas** were a prominent South Indian dynasty who rose to power after the decline of the Satavahanas in the 3rd century A.D.
● Tamil society witnessed a great change during the Pallava period. ○ The **caste system became rigid**, with **Brahmins occupying a high place** in society. ○ They were given **land grants by kings and nobles** and were also entrusted with the responsibility of looking after temples. ○ The period also saw the **rise of Saivism and Vaishnavism** and the **decline of Buddhism and** **Jainism**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Pallava art and architecture represent an **early stage of Dravidian art and architecture** which blossomed to its fullest extent under the Chola Dynasty. The f**irst stone and mortar temples of South India were** **constructed** during Pallava rule. **So, statements III and IV are correct.**
● The Pallavas were **great patrons of learning**. Their capital **Kanchi, was an ancient centre of learning**. The **Ghatika at Kanchi** attracted students from all parts of India and abroad. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The central government was responsible for providing **irrigation facilities**, and several irrigation tanks, such as those at **Mahendravadi and Mamandoor**, were dug during Mahendravarman I’s reign. **So, all four of the statements given above are correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
In the context of ancient India, ‘Dinganaga’ and
‘Bharavi’ were:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Pallavas** were great patrons of learning, and their capital Kanchi, emerged as a renowned centre of education. The **Ghatika at Kanchi** became a popular institution, drawing students from all over India and even from abroad.
● Notable personalities associated with Kanchi include **Mayurasarman**, founder of the Kadamba dynasty, who studied the Vedas there, **Dinganaga, the Buddhist writer,** and **Dharmapala,** who later headed **Nalanda University** and belonged to Kanchi.
● **Bharavi**, the eminent **Sanskrit scholar**, lived during the reign of **Simhavishnu**, while Dandin, another celebrated Sanskrit writer, adorned the court of Narasimhavarman II.
● **Tamil literature** flourished with the **Nayanmars** and **Alwars** composing religious hymns such as the **Devaram** and **Nalayradivyaprabandam**, representing the spiritual literature of the Pallava era. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Mahendravarman
I - of the Pallava
dynasty composed the Sanskrit play
Mattavilasaprahasanam.
II. 	 Dharmapala composed the religious literature
Devaram.
III. 	Nandivarman II translated the Mahabharata
as Bharathavenba in Tamil.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Pallavas** were great patrons of **learning**. **Mahendravarman I** himself composed the Sanskrit play **Mattavilasaprahasanam**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Devaram,** composed by Nayanmars, and the Nalayradivyaprabandam composed by Alwars, represent the religious literature of the Pallava period. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Perundevanar** was patronized by **Nandivarman II** and translated the **Mahabharata** as **Bharathavenba** in Tamil. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● **Nandikkalambagam** was another important work, but the name of the author of this work is not known. Music and dance also developed during this period. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 The defeat of Harshavardhana
II. 	 Establishment of control over Kashmir and
Nepal
III. 	The conquest of Sri Lanka
IV. 	 Construction of the Gangaikonda Cholapuram
temple
V. 	 Extension of sovereignty over Kadambas and
Gangas
How many of the above events occurred during the reign of Pulakesin II?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Pulakesin II** (608-642 A.D.) was the most renowned ruler of the **Chalukya dynasty**.
● The most important military encounter in the reign of **Pulakesin II**, the Western Chalukya ruler, was **his** **celebrated victory over Harshavardhana.** Harshavardhana, seeking to expand his kingdom south of the Narmada river, marched against Pulakesin II. However, Pulakesin II successfully defeated Harshavardhana, a feat prominently recorded in the **Aihole inscription**, where Pulakesin II assumed the proud title of **Paramesvara** following his triumph. **So, point I is correct.**
● Pulakesin II established his suzerainty over the **Kadambas of Banavasi** and the Gangas of Mysore. The **Ganga ruler Durvinita accepted his overlordship** and cemented ties by giving his daughter in marriage to Pulakesin II. **So, point V is correct.**
● Pulakesin II was initially victorious against the Pallavas in his first expedition but later suffered a humiliating defeat at the hands of Narasimhavarman I near Kanchi.
● **Nepal had accepted Harsha’s overlordship**. Harsha established his control over **Kashmir** and its ruler sent tributes to him. He also maintained cordial relations with Bhaskaravarman, the ruler of Assam. **So,** **point II is correct.**
● Mahinda V, the king of Sri Lanka attempted to recover from the Cholas the northern part of Ceylon. **Rajendra I** defeated him and seized southern Sri Lanka. Thus the whole of Sri Lanka was made part of the Chola Empire. **So, point III is not correct.**
● Rajendra I defeated Mahipala I of Bengal. To commemorate this successful north-Indian campaign he founded the city of **Gangaikondacholapuram** and constructed the famous Rajesvaram temple in that city. He also excavated a large irrigation tank called Cholagangam on the western side of the city. **So,** **point IV is not correct.** **So, only two of the above events occurred during the reign of Pulakesin II.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which of the following paintings are depicted in
the Ajanta caves?
I. 	 The dying princess
II. 	 Bodhisattva Padmapani
III. 	Varahadeva
IV. 	 Scenes from Panchatantra tales
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Ajanta Caves** are a series of rock-cut Buddhist caves located in the **Sahyadri** **ranges** on the banks of the **Waghora River**, near Aurangabad in Maharashtra. The caves showcase the life of the Buddha, his teachings, and moral stories, often using Jataka tales to illustrate these themes.
● **Examples of Ajanta art** include the **Dying Princess** and **Flying Apsara** frescoes, highlighting the sophisticated artistic techniques and themes of Indian mural painting during this era. **So, point I is correct.**
● In Mahayana Buddhism, the Bodhisattva **Avalokiteshvara** was also known as **Padmapani** (**Holder of the Lotus**) or Lokesvara (Lord of the World). Avalokiteshvara is a Bodhisattva who embodies compassion for all Buddhas. This Bodhisattva is variably depicted, described and is portrayed in different cultures as either female or male. The painting of Bodhisattva Padmapani is the most famous and oft-illustrated paintings at **Ajanta**. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Varahadeva** (patron of Cave No. 16), who was the prime minister of the Vakataka king, Harishena. **So, point III is correct.**
● The **Panchatantra** is a collection of Indian fables with animal characters and moral lessons; it **is not the subject of the Ajanta paintings.  So,** **point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Ajanta Caves:**
● The paintings in the Ajanta Caves are famous for their use of the **fresco technique**.
● The outlines were drawn in **red**, and the interiors were then colored, with a striking **absence of blue** in the palette.
● There are a total of **29 caves**, of which **25 served as Viharas** (residential caves) and **4** **as Chaityas** (prayer halls). The development of these caves took place between **200 BC** **and 650 AD**. The caves were mainly inscribed and painted by **Buddhist monks** under the **patronage of Vakataka kings**, notably **Harishena**.
● The art at Ajanta demonstrates **considerable naturalism** and is themed around **Buddhism**, depicting scenes from the **life of Buddha** and the **Jataka tales**. Of the 29
caves, **five belong to the Hinayana phase** and **the remaining 24 to the Mahayana** **phase** of Buddhism.
● **Cave No. 16** stands out as an elegant specimen of cave architecture. Notable sculptures include the **Mahaparinirvana of Buddha in Cave No. 26** and the **Naga King and his** **consort in Cave No. 19**.
● References to Ajanta can also be found in the travel accounts of Chinese pilgrims **Fa** **Hien and Hiuen Tsang**.
● A famous **Ajanta cave painting depicts Pulakesin II**, the Chalukya king, **receiving an** **embassy from the Persian king Khusrau II**. This painting, found in **Cave 1,** is a visual record of the diplomatic exchange between the Chalukya and Sasanian empires.
● The caves contain a famous fresco **depicting Buddha accepting a bowl of rice from** **his son Rahula,** while his wife Yasodhara looks on with a mix of sorrow and contemplation. This scene, found in **Cave 17,** portrays Buddha's return to his former home in Kapilavastu and his encounter with his family after becoming enlightened.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Dynasty Founder
I. - Chola Vijayalaya
II. - Pushyabhuti Harshavardhan
III. - Gupta Samudragupta
IV. - Chalukya Pulakeshin I
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Chola dynasty** re-emerged as a significant power in South India with the rise of **Vijayalaya Chola** (850–871 CE), who captured the Kaveri delta from the Muttaraiyars and established the Chola kingdom in **850 CE.** Vijayalaya’s successors, beginning with **Parantaka I** (907–955 CE), expanded the kingdom’s territory and administrative base. The dynasty reached its peak under **Rajaraja I** (985–1014 CE), who built the grand **Brihadishvara Temple** at Thanjavur, and his son **Rajendra I** (1012–1044 CE), who led successful naval expeditions reaching as far as the **Srivijaya Empire** in Southeast Asia. **So, pair I is** **correctly matched.**
● The **Vardhana (Pushyabhuti) dynasty** was founded by **Pushyabhuti**, who ruled from **Thaneswar** and initially served as a military general under the **imperial Guptas**. He rose to power after the decline of the Guptas. The dynasty gained strength under his descendant Prabhakaravardhana (580–605 CE), who successfully fought against the Gurjaras and the Huns, extending his authority as far as Malwa and Gujarat. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **Samudragupta** was one of the greatest emperors of the Gupta dynasty and is often referred to as the Napoleon of India due to his extensive military conquests. However, he was not the founder. **The founder** **of the Gupta dynasty was Sri Gupta (240–280 CE),** who laid the foundation of what would become one of ancient India's most significant empires. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **The Chalukya dynasty** emerged as a significant power in the Deccan region under its **founder** **Pulakeshin I (c. 535–566 CE),** who fortified a hill near Badami and declared independence from the Kadambas. While he laid the foundation of the dynasty, the capital city **Badami** was formally established by his son Kirtivarman I (566–597 CE). The dynasty reached new heights under Pulakesin II (609–642 CE), the grandson of Pulakeshin I. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Chola Ruler Architectural Contribution
I. - Rajaraja I Built Brihadeshwara at Thanjavur
II. - Rajendra I Built an irrigation tank called Cholagangam
III. - Kulothunga I Built Vimana of Nataraja temple
IV. - Rajadhiraia Planted a victory pillar at Yadgir
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**The Chola dynasty** (circa 9th–13th centuries CE) is celebrated as one of the greatest patrons of temple architecture in South India, heralding an era of grand stone temples that shaped the Dravidian style
● **Rajaraja I,** one of the greatest rulers of the Chola dynasty, was a significant patron of art and architecture. His most famous contribution is the construction of the **Brihadeshwara Temple** at **Thanjavur**, completed around 1010 CE. This grand Shiva temple, built in the Dravidian architectural style, stands as a testament to Chola engineering and artistic excellence. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Rajendra I**, the son and successor of Rajaraja I, carried forward his father's legacy and expanded the Chola Empire. To commemorate his victorious campaign up to the Ganges, he established a new capital named **Gangaikondacholapuram**. He **built an irrigation tank called Cholagangam** near the capital called Jala-stambha (water-pillar). It became the coronation centre, which was a Chola landmark. **So, pair** **II is correctly matched.**
● **Parantaka I,** a devout Shaivite and an early ruler of the Chola dynasty, ruled for forty-eight years and played a significant role in strengthening Chola power in Tamil Nadu. One of his most notable contributions wa**s building the vimana** (tower above the sanctum) **of the Nataraja temple at** **Chidambaram with gold**. This temple, dedicated to Lord Nataraja (Shiva as the cosmic dancer), is an important Shaivite shrine and one of the five elemental lingas revered in the Shaivism tradition. **So, pair** **III is not correctly matched.**
● **Rajadhiraia** was a powerful Chola ruler who suppressed rebellions in Pandya, Kerala and Ceylon, and undertook a major expedition to restore Chola authority in Vengi. Rajadhiraja won a decisive battle at Pundur on the banks of Krishna. **To commemorate these victories, he erected a Jayastambha (victory** **pillar) at Yetagiri (modern-day Yadgir) bearing the Chola tiger emblem.** This symbolic act asserted Chola supremacy in enemy territory.  **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, only three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer**.
**Knowledge Box**
● **Kulottunga I** was the grandson of Rajendra I through his daughter Ammangadevi. He succeeded to the Chola throne and thus united the Vengi kingdom with the Chola Empire. **During his reign Sri Lanka became independent.** Subsequently, Vengi and the Mysore region were captured by the western Chalukyas. Kulottunga I sent a large embassy of 72 merchants to China and maintained cordial relations with the kingdom of Sri Vijaya.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following inscriptions functions
like a local constitution for the gram sabha,
detailing its functioning, member qualifications,
election process and grounds for disqualification?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In ancient India, **inscriptions** served as vital historical records engraved on stone or metal plates, detailing political events, administrative orders, land grants, and socio-economic conditions.
● The inscription referred to is the **Uttaramerur inscription**, located in present-day Kanchipuram district of Tamil Nadu. It dates back to the reign of Chola king **Parantaka I** (907–953 AD) and is inscribed on the walls of the **Vaikunda Perumal Temple**.
● This inscription functions like a **local constitution for the gram sabha, providing detailed guidelines** **on how the assembly should operate**. It outlines the qualifications for members, such as land ownership, age limits, and knowledge of Vedic texts; describes the process of selection through a draw of lots; and lists conditions for disqualification, including criminal activity, misconduct, or failure to submit accounts. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Which of the following foreign regions were
controlled by the Chola empire?
I. 	 Malaya
II. 	 Ceylon
III. 	China
IV. 	 Sumatra
V. 	 Java
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:

options.b: I, II, IV, and V only
options.d: I, II, III, IV, and V

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● During its imperial phase, the **Chola Empire** **under rulers like Rajaraja I and Rajendra I** undertook extensive military and naval expeditions that extended their influence beyond the Indian subcontinent. They **successfully conquered Ceylon** (modern-day Sri Lanka) and launched expeditions to Southeast Asia, **bringing parts of the Malaya** **Peninsula, Sumatra and Java under their** **control or influence.** These campaigns were part of the Cholas’ effort to control key maritime trade routes. **So, points I, II, IV and V are correct.**
● **China was never conquered by the Cholas.** While there were diplomatic and trade exchanges with Chinese courts, no military conquest or political control over China occurred. **So, point III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Battle Result
I. - Battle of Manimangalam,
Narasimhavarman I captured Chalukyan capital Vatapi
II. - Battle of Vellur,
Parantaka I captured Madurai
III. - Battle of Takkolam, 949
Rashtrakutas captured northern territories of the Chola
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Battle of Manimangalam, 642 AD** was a significant conflict fought between the **Pallavas** and the **Chalukyas** during the reign of **Narasimhavarman I**, who ruled from 630 to 668 CE. Known by the title ‘Mamalla’ meaning Great Wrestler, Narasimhavarman I successfully repelled the invasion of Pulakesin II, the powerful Chalukyan king, at Manimangalam, near Kanchi. It involved sacking of the Chalukyan capital, Vatapi. In recognition of this achievement, Narasimhavarman I assumed the title **‘Vatapikonda’**, meaning conqueror of Vatapi. **So, pair I is correctly matched**
● The **Battle of Vellur, 910** was a significant military encounter between **Parantaka I of the Chola** **dynasty and Pandya ruler Rajasimha**. After defeating the Pandya ruler Rajasimha and capturing Madurai, Parantaka assumed the title **‘Maduraikonda’** (Conqueror of Madurai). However, the Pandya king fled to the court of the Sri Lankan monarch, forming an alliance against the Cholas. Parantaka I decisively defeated the combined forces of the Pandyas and the king of Sri Lanka in the Battle of Vellur, securing Chola dominance over the Pandyan territories. **So, pair II is correctly matched**
● The **Battle of Takkolam, fought in 949 CE, was a pivotal military conflict between the Cholas and** **the Rashtrakutas**. During the later years of Parantaka I’s reign, the Chola Empire began to face serious threats from the north. Krishna III, one of the most powerful Rashtrakuta rulers, led an aggressive campaign against the Cholas and inflicted a decisive defeat on them at Takkolam, located near presentday Arkonam in Tamil Nadu. This battle marked a major setback for the Cholas, as the **Rashtrakutas**
**succeeded in occupying a significant portion of the northern territories** of the Chola Empire. **So, pair** **III is correctly matched** **So,  all three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following was referred to as a
‘Chola Lake’ due to expansion of the Cholas in maritime trade and Naval dominance?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Cholas (850−1200 CE) came to power after overthrowing the authority of the Pallavas in South India.
● The Cholas paid special attention to their navy. The n**aval achievements of the Tamils reached its** **climax under the Cholas**. They controlled the Malabar and Coromandal coasts. In fact, the **Bay of Bengal** **became a Chola lake for some time.**
● The Cholas built and maintained a strong navy which allowed them to control the maritime trade routes that traversed the Bay of Bengal, linking South India with Southeast Asia (like the Srivijaya Empire of Indonesia and Malaysia).
● The **Chola king** **Rajendra I in 1025 CE, even sent a naval expedition against Malaya** and neighbouring countries to overcome their interference in the trade with China. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Expansion of Chola Empire in Ganges Plain:**
● Rajendra I (c.1014−1044 CE) in 1022 CE, led an expedition to the north, crossed the Ganga. He defeated the Pala ruler, Mahipal I and the Western Chalukyas.
● To commemorate this occasion, **he assumed the title of** **Gangaikondachola** (meaning ‘the Chola conqueror of Ganga’)
● He also **established a new capital named Gangaikondacholapuram** (meaning ‘the city of the Chola conqueror of the Ganga’) near the mouth of the river Kaveri.
● He built a Shiva temple here and excavated a tank called Chodagarg.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
In the context of the history of southern India, the
term ‘Kadagams’ refers to:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**:
● **Kadagams or padaividu** were the military cantonments where the Chola army maintained its troops and got trained. These were well-organized and often fortified. Kadagams were used for **defending** **territories, launching campaigns**, and maintaining control over conquered regions.
● The **soldiers of the Cholas generally consisted of two types**—the **kaikkolar** who were royal troops receiving regular pay from the treasury; and the **nattuppadai** who were the militia men employed only for local defence. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Which of the following were the major items of
export from the Chola Empire?
I. 	 Textiles
II. 	 Spices
III. 	Ivory
IV. 	 Camphor Choose the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: d** **Explanation**: **Economic life in the Chola empire:** **Land tax** constituted the single largest source of income of the Chola state. It was generally assessed at one-third of the produce. The village assembly took land tax and local levies. Cattle rearing was a subsidiary occupation.
● **Trade with foreign countries was an important** **feature of the Cholas mercantile activities**. The rulers built a network of royal roads that were useful for trade as well as for the movement of the army. There were **gigantic trade guilds that** **traded with Java and Sumatra.**
● South India exported **textiles**, **spices**, drugs, jewels, **ivory**, horn, ebony and **camphor** to China. Trade brought considerable prestige and affluence to the Cholas. Kalanju was the currency prevalent in the Chola kingdom. **So, points I, II, III and IV** **are correct.**
● The department of land revenue is known as **puravuvari-tinaikkalam**, with its chief called 'puravuvaritinaikkalanayagam'. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs with regard to the
Chola empire:
Social Group Occupation
I. - Idangai Artisan
II. - Valangai Merchant
III. - Vellavar Landed aristocracy
IV. - Kaikolar Weaver
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: d** **Explanation** The **industrial population during the Chola period was broadly divided into the valangai (right-hand) and** **idangai (left-hand) divisions**. The Chola kingdom often witnessed feuds between these two groups.
● The **'Idangai'** (Left-Hand) division comprised various **artisanal groups**, agricultural laborers, and other service-oriented communities. They generally occupied a lower social standing. **So, pair I is correctly** **matched.**
● The **'Valangai'** (Right-Hand) division typically consisted of dominant agricultural communities (like the Vellalars), **merchants**, and certain categories of artisans. They generally held a higher social and economic status. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● The **Vellalar** occupied one of the highest positions in society and formed the nobility or the **landed** **aristocracy** of the country and who were economically a powerful group. **So, pair III is correctly** **matched.**
● The **Kaikolar community were weavers and merchants,** but they also maintained armies. During the Chola period they had predominant trading and military roles. Kadaisiyar were landless laborers who worked in the fields.
