They were at the very bottom of the agrarian social hierarchy. **So, pair IV is correctly** **matched.** **So, all four of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Important City during Sangam age
Known For
I. - Puhar Trading port
II. - Arikamedu Arretine Wares
III. Madurai Gathering of two Sangam Councils
IV. - Kanchipuram Capital city of Early Cholas and the Pallavas
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Chola kingdom** of Sangam period extended up to Venkatam (Tirupathi hills). The Kaveri delta region remained the central part of the kingdom. This area was later known as Cholamandalam. **Their** **port** **Puhar, also known as Kaveripattinam, attracted merchants** from various regions of the Indian Ocean. It was located on the **east coast** of India. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
○ The Pattinapaalai, a poetic work in the Pathinenkeezhkanakku, gives elaborate information of the trading activity during the rule of Karikalan.
● **Arikamedu**, a coastal settlement in present-day Puducherry on the banks of the Ariyankuppam river, was an important trading center during the Chola period. Excavations at the site have uncovered substantial
**Points to Remember**
● The **use** **of** **agrahatta** **(Persian wheel)** facilitated the already established smooth irrigation networks of tanks, canals, wells and sluices.
evidence of a Roman trading settlement including amphorae, lamps, glassware, coins, beads made of stone, glass and gold, and gems. This Indo-Roman trading city is well known for **Arretine Ware,** a type of stamped, red-glazed pottery that originated in Arretium (modern-day Arezzo), Italy, during the Roman Empire. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Madurai** was the capital of Pandyas. Madurai is the place where the **two Sangam Councils** (first and third) were gathered. The recorded history of the city goes back to the 3rd century BC. Madurai as a city was mentioned by Megasthenese and Kautilya. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Kanchipuram** is one of the seven holiest ancient cities of India and was also called “Kanchiyur” in the Sangam period. Kanchipuram traces its history to the 2**nd century BCE as an early Chola capital**. From the 3rd to the 9th century CE, it was the **Pallava capital**, and from the 10th to the 13th century, it served as the late Chola government headquarters. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.**
○ The capital of the Later Chola Empire was initially **Thanjavur** (Tanjore), and then **Gangaikonda** **Cholapuram**. **So, all four of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Sangam Councils:**
● The **first Sangam** had its seat in old **Madurai** now submerged in the Indian ocean. It is believed that the first Sangam was attended by gods and legendary sages, but all works have perished, no less than 4,499 authors submitted their writings to the Sangam and obtained its approval. Eighty nine Pandyan kings, seven of them poets, patronised the first Sangam which lasted for 4400 years. The standard works of the first Sangam were Agattiyam, Paripadal, Mudanarai, Mudukuruku and Kalariavirai.
● The **second Sangam** had its seat in another submerged town called **Kapatapuram**. It was attended by 3700 poets, and received the support of fifty nine Pandyan kings and flourished in unbroken continuity for 3700 years. The works of the second Sangam are also now lost except **Tolkappiyam**, the great Tamil grammar attributed to Tolkappiyar, one of Agastya’s twelve direct disciples.
○ Kapatapuram served as the second capital of the Pandyan kingdom
● The **third Sangam** had its seat in **Northern Madura (Madurai)** founded by Mudatirumaran. Its membership totaled forty nine, but 449 poets submitted their works to it for approval. Forty nine Pandyan kings patronised it and continued for 1850 years. While many of the works produced in this Sangam are lost, some have fortunately survived to give modern readers an idea of the richness and variety of Sangam literature. Some of the principal works of the third Sangam have come down to us in the shape of anthologies. Of these three are famous. **Pathuppattu** (the ten Idylls), **Ettuthogai** (the eight Collections) and **Padinenkilkanakku** (the eighteen Minor Didactic Poems).

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the  Udayagiri and Khandagiri Caves of Odisha:
I. 	 They were carved by Chalukyan rulers.
II.  	 The caves have both artificial and natural
caves.
III. 	They are famous for the Hathigumpha
inscription, carved out in Brahmi script.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**The Udayagiri and Khandagiri Caves (Odisha):**
● These caves were carved in the first or second century BC close to the present-day Bhubaneswar under the Kalinga King Kharavela.
● The caves have both artificial and natural caves (possibly carved out as residence of Jain monks).
● The caves are famous for the **Hathigumpha inscription**, carved out in **Brahmi** script, which starts out with **“Jain Namokar Mantra”** and highlights various military campaigns undertaken by King Kharavela.
● The double-storey **Ranigumpha cave** in Udayagiri is home to some exquisite sculptures. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Chera dynasty:
I. 	 Its empire contained parts of both Kerala and
Tamil Nadu.
II. 	 Cheran Senguttuvan belonged to this dynasty.
III. 	It introduced the Pattini cult, the worship of
Kannagi as the ideal wife.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Chera or the Kerala country was situated to the west and north of the land of the Pandyas. It included the narrow strip of land between the sea and the mountains and covered **portions of both Kerala and** **Tamilnadu**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Cheran Senguttuvan** of the Chera dynasty belonged to the second century A.D. His younger brother was Elango Adigal, the author of Silappathigaram. Among his military achievements, his **expedition to the** **Himalayas** was remarkable. He defeated many north Indian monarchs. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Senguttuvan introduced the **Pattini cult (Kannagi cult)** or the worship of Kannagi as the ideal wife in Tamil Nadu, which is worship of the **goddess of Chastity.** **So, statement III is correct.**
● The stone for making the **idol of Kannagi** was brought by him after his Himalayan expedition. The consecration ceremony was attended by many princes including Gajabhagu II from Sri Lanka. **Therefore,** **option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
In the Sangam society, agriculture was a chief
occupation for which one of the following divisions?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Tolkappiyam** refers to the five-fold division of lands during the Sangam Society - Kurinji (hilly tracks), Mullai (pastoral), Marudam (agricultural), Neydal (coastal) and Palai (desert).
● The people living in these five divisions had their respective chief occupations as well as gods for worship. ○ **Kurinji**: Chief deity was **Murugan** – chief occupation was **hunting and honey collection.** ○ **Mullai**: Chief deity was **Mayon (Vishnu) –** chief occupation was **cattle-rearing and dealing with** **dairy products.** ○ **Marudam**: Chief deity was **Indira** – chief occupation was **agriculture**. ○ **Neydal**: Chief deity was **Varunan** – chief occupation was **fishing and salt manufacturing.** ○ **Palai**: Chief deity was **Korravai** – chief occupation was **robbery**. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **What information does the Tolkappiyam text give about the sangam society?**
● Tolkappiyam also refers to four castes namely **arasar, anthanar, vanigar and** **vellalar.**
○ The ruling class was called arasar. ○ Anthanars played a significant role in the Sangam polity and religion. ○ Vanigars carried on trade and commerce. ○ The vellalas were agriculturists.
● Other tribal groups like Parathavar, Panar, Eyinar, Kadambar, Maravar and Pulaiyar were also found in the Sangam society.
● Ancient primitive tribes like Thodas, Irulas, Nagas and Vedars lived in this period.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
In the context of ancient Indian history, ‘Avvaiyar’,
‘Nachchellaiyar’, and ‘Kakkaipadiniyar’ were:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Sangam literature provides ample evidence to trace the **position of women** during the Sangam age.
● **Women poets** flourished in this period and contributed to Tamil literature. From Sangam literature, we find the names of **26 poetesses**: **Avvaiyar, Nachchellaiyar, Kakkaipadiniyar**, Pottiyar, Uttiyar, Budappandiyan Devi, Nannakaiyar, Okkur Masattiyar, etc., to name a few. The courage of women was also appreciated in many poems.
○ **Avvaiyar** was the most renowned poetess. She has written 58 poems and also ethical works such as **Niti-Venba, Niti-Neri-Vilakkam, Ulaka-Niti, Nanneri****, etc**.
● **Karpu** or Chaste life was considered the highest virtue of women. **Love marriage** was a common practice. Women were allowed to choose their life partners.
● However, the **life of widows** was miserable. The practice of Sati was also prevalent in the higher strata of society. The **class of dancers** was patronized by the kings and nobles. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Chola Empire:
I. 	 Queen Sembiyan Mahadevi is depicted as the
goddess Parvati in a bronze sculpture.
II. 	 Princess Kundavai patronised the Kunthavai
Jinalaya Jain temple complex in Tirumalai.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Women in the time of the Imperial Cholas** seem to have held a position much different from the sangam age. Women in the Chola period have made **enduring positive contributions**. In general, ranging from the royal women to the women of the lower strata, they had contributed to the socio-economic, political and cultural contribution of the country.
● Royal women played a prominent role in society. The Queens were generally known as Madeviyar, Deviyar, Madevadigal, Kokkilanadigal; the chief queen was known as Agramahadevi, Tribhuvanamadevi and Logamadevi.
● **Sembiyan Mahadevi:** She was a 10th century CE Chola queen, who was the wife of Gandaraditya Chola and the **mother of** **Uttama Chola.** When Parantaka I died in 950 CE, Gandaraditya became king and Sembiyan Mahadevi the queen.
○ She was dedicated to **renovating old temples**, **building** **new ones and initiating public works**. She did more with Gandaraditya’s support, when he became king. ○ She was a pioneer in **Kalpani (literally ‘stone-** **work’)** or projects involving conversion of old temples of brick and mortar into endurable granite temples. ○ She had a farsighted appreciation of history – she ordered that the **ancient inscriptions be copied** first before reconstructing the temple in granite. The temples and icons commissioned by her had **her** **unique stamp**. ○ She **donated jewels and bronzes** to many temples – many commissioned by her, others by her family members like Rajaraja and Uttama.A **Chola** **bronze** of the 10th century portrays her as goddess **Parvati** herself. **So, statement I is correct.**
**Points To Remember**
● **Sati** was not a standard operating practice in Chola tradition, though some queens practised it. For instance, **Queen Vanavan Mahadevi,** Sundara Chola’s wife, practised sati in 970 CE. So did **Queen Viramahadevi,** when Rajendra Chola died in 1044 CE.
○ **Princess Kundavai:** Kundavai Pirattiyar, **Rajaraja’s elder sister**, was married to Vallavarayan Vandiyathevan, the Bana ruler of Brahmadesam, a feudatory of the Cholas, and also a powerful commander of the Chola army. ○ From here she chose to serve public causes. She **contributed gold and jewels to the** **Brihadeeswara Temple**. ○ Kundavai’s contributions were the most important being to the Jain shrines of **Darasuram (or** **Rajarajeswaram) and Tirumalai (near Tiruvannamalai)**. She gave away lands for building hospitals, and donated money for irrigation projects – like the **Kundavai-pereri** at Brahmadesam. ○ Kundavai is credited with **patronizing the Kunthavai Jinalaya Jain temple complex in** **Tirumalai, Tamil Nadu**. This 10th-century temple is also known as the Kundavai Jainalaya. It's one of two Jain temple sites commissioned by her, the other being in **Dadapuram. So, statement** **II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the fine arts of the Sangam Age:
I. 	 Yazh was a prominent stringed musical
instrument.
II. 	 Panar and Viraliyar were the singing bards in
the royal court.
III. 	Kanigaiyar were professional female dancers
who performed in public spaces.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Sangam literature mentions that music, dance, and poetry were an integral part of life in royal courts and public places. **Poetry, music and dancing** were popular among the people of the Sangam age. Liberal donations were given to poets by the kings, chieftains and nobles.
● The arts of music and dancing were highly developed. A variety of **Yazhs and drums** are referred to in the Sangam literature.
● The **yazh** was a prominent stringed musical instrument during the Sangam period. It is described as an ancient Tamil harp, considered one of the **earliest forms of the violin**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The royal courts were crowded with **singing bards called Panar and Viraliyar**. They were experts in folk songs and folk dances. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Aham literature** of the sangam age gave ample references to the **courtesans** as a separate class of persons. They were called parattaiyar or **Kanigaiyar**. They were **professional female dancers** who performed in royal courts and other public spaces during the Sangam period. **Koothu** was the most popular entertainment of the people. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Bhakti Saint Composition
I. - Manikkavachakar Gnaneswari
II. - Basavanna Vachanas
III. - Andal Ramcharitmanas
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Bhakti Movement originated in South India, with the Alvars (Vaishnavite saints) and Nayanars (Shaivite saints) between the 6th and 9th centuries CE. It was a significant religious and social movement advocating personal devotion (bhakti) to a single deity as the path to salvation, transcending ritualistic practices, caste distinctions, and rigid orthodoxy. It later spread to North India between the 13th and 17th centuries, taking various regional forms and expressions
● **Gnanadeva** was the founder of the Bhakti Movement in Maharashtra in the thirteenth century. It was called Maharashtra dharma. He wrote a commentary of Bhagavat Gita called **Gnaneswari**. **So, pair I is** **not correctly matched.**
○ **Manikkavachakar:** He was a 9th-century Tamil Shaiva poet-saint, whose famous work Tiruvachakam expresses deep **personal devotion and surrender to Lord Shiva**. His hymns are noted for intense bhakti and mystical experiences.
● **Basavanna and his vachanas:** He was a 12th-century philosopher and social reformer from Karnataka. His **vachanas in Kannada addressed social equality**, rejected caste hierarchies, and emphasised devotion (bhakti) to Lord Shiva. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Andal:** She was the **only female Alvar saint of the Tamil Bhakti tradition**. Her compositions express intense love and longing for Lord Vishnu, especially as Krishna, symbolising mystical union with the divine.
○ Tulsidas was a worshipper of Rama and composed the famous **Ramcharitmanas**, the Hindi version of Ramayana. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.** **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With regard to the religious practices in India,
the “Yapaniya” sect belongs to which one of the following?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Yapaniya** developed as a **Jaina sect** around the first century A.D. It was probably founded by Kalasa, a monk of **Svetambara** sect at Kalayanagara.
● A significant feature of this sect was that it thought that even **women could attain moksha** and that kevalins should take their food in morsels.
● The Yapaniyas worshipped nude images which still exist and the people who worship in these temples nowadays are Digambaras.
● **It existed up to 14th century A.D**. and ultimately absorbed itself into the Digambara community. The earliest reference of this sect is made probably in the inscription of Kharavela in the 2nd Century A.D. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Bhakti movement in India,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Saguna bhakti emphasises devotion to a
personal deity with attributes and form.
II. 	 The Nirguna bhakti emphasises internal
spiritual realisation instead of idol worship.
III. 	Both Saguna and Nirguna bhakti aim for
spiritual liberation and a deeper connection with the divine.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Bhakti tradition in India is a devotional movement that emphasised intense personal devotion (bhakti) to a chosen deity as the primary means of attaining moksha (liberation), as opposed to the rigid ritualism of Vedic orthodoxy.
● **The Saguna Bhakti tradition believes in devotion to a personal God with a name, form, and divine** **attributes, such as Vishnu (particularly as Rama or Krishna) or Shiva**. Devotees in this tradition engaged in temple worship, idol veneration, and ritual practices. Saints like Tulsidas, who wrote Ramcharitmanas, and Surdas, known for his Krishna bhakti poems, exemplify this tradition. The use of icons, temple singing, pilgrimages, and rituals was accepted as a legitimate path to liberation in this stream of Bhakti. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **The Nirguna Bhakti tradition advocates devotion to a formless, attributeless, and all-pervading** **divine reality, often identified with Brahman or Paramatma**. Saints like Kabir, Guru Nanak, Ravidas, and Dadu Dayal rejected ritualism, caste-based discrimination, and idol worship. Instead, they emphasised inner purity, moral living, meditation on the divine name (nam-simran), and the unity of all faiths. Their teachings promoted social equality, universality of God, and spiritual introspection, and many of their verses are compiled in texts like the Guru Granth Sahib. **So, statements II and III are correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following anthologies is a major
composition by the Alvars, generally described as the ‘Tamil Veda’?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Nalayira Divya Prabandham** is a revered compilation of 4,000 Tamil hymns composed by the twelve **Alvars**, the celebrated Vaishnava poet-saints of South India. The collection was systematized and preserved in its current form by Nathamuni during the 9th–10th centuries CE.
● Due to its spiritual authority and deep influence in Tamil religious culture, the Nalayira Divya Prabandham is often referred to as the **“Dravida Veda” or the “Tamil Veda**”, reflecting its parallel status to the Sanskrit Vedas in South Indian Vaishnavism.
● The hymns were composed and sung by the Alvars in states of **devotional ecstasy at various Vishnu** **temples**, many of which came to be celebrated as Divya Desams or Magalashasana Sthalas—shrines glorified through their verses. These compositions are devoted to Lord Vishnu and his avatars, especially **Rama and Krishna.**
● They are known for their **literary elegance**, intense devotion, and deep theological insights, making them f**oundational to the Sri Vaishnava tradition**. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Bhakti tradition in South
India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 While Alvars were Vaishnava poet-saints,
Nayanars were the devotees of lord Shiva.
II. 	 Both the Alvars and Nayanars were opposed
to Brahmanical domination.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In South India, there were two main groups of Bhakti saints: **Nayanars (Shiva devotees)** and **Alvars** **(Vishnu Devotees)** who preached devotion to God as a means of salvation and disregarded the austerities preached by Buddhism and Jainism.
● The **Alvars were Vaishnava poet-saints** who sang praises of Vishnu or his avatar Krishna. They were propagators of Vaishnavism and regarded Vishnu or Krishna as the supreme being. Andal was the only female Alvar saint and is referred to as ‘Meera of the South’.
● Nayanars were originally a group of 63 Tamil saints **devoted to Lord Shiva,** who adopted extreme asceticism to attain the larger goal. The three Nayanars **Appar, Sambandar and Sundarar** compiled a collection of poems called **Tevaram** in 10th century AD. Among nayanars were Brahmins and nobles but they also included the oil-mongers and the Vellalas. The Alvars and Nayanars were **opposed to** **Brahmanical domination and were also opposed to Jainism and Buddhism. So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **Nayanars’ hymns are compiled in the Tirumurai**, which consists of 12 volumes. There were 12 Alvars who composed a text **“Nalariya Divya Prabandham”, which included Alvars’ hymns** (4000 verses). **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct
with regard to the Ikshvaku dynasty?
I. 	 It was founded by Sri Chintamula.
II. 	 It was the successor dynasty to the Satavahanas.
III. 	It ruled over the Krishna-Guntur region.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● After the fall of the Satavahana empire, **3rd to the 4th century CE,** the empire was partitioned among minor dynasties. In Deccan and Western India (around Nasik) the Abhiras established themselves; the **Ikshvakus** carved out for themselves a kingdom in the **eastern (Krishna-Guntur) region**. Therefore, Ikshvaku and Abhiras were contemporary to each other. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Sri Chintamula** was the founder of the Ikshvakus dynasty, he issued **Ashvamedha & Elephant type** **coins** as well. However, Ikshvaku dynasty’s coins were primarily lead coins characterized by an elephant symbol on one side and the **Ujjain symbol** on the other. **So, statements I and III are correct.**
● The Ikshvakus were **Shaivites and** followers of the Brahmanical tradition, while at the same time the women of the royal household came from chiefly families patronising the Buddhist religion. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the scripts of ancient India,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 Brahmi was written from right to left while
Kharosthi from left to right.
II. 	 Nagari, Sharda and Siddham scripts are
derived from the Brahmi script.
III. 	Aramaic script originally belonged to the
southern part of the Indian subcontinent.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Kharoshti script:** It was a writing system used in northwestern India before about **500 CE.** The earliest extant inscription in Kharoshti dates from **251 BCE,** and the latest dates from the **4th–5th century CE.** Unlike Brahmi which was written from left to right like Hindi, **Kharosthi was written from right to left.** **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Brahmi script:** It is ancestral to all Indian scripts except Kharoṣṭhi. The Brahmi script was **first** **deciphered in 1837 by James Princep**. It can be traced to the 8th or 7th century BC. A later, more refined, version of the Brahmi script, called **Gupta Brahmi**, was widely used between the fourth and sixth centuries AD. The Gupta Brahmi script then evolved into three separate scripts: **Nagari, Sharda and** **Siddham. So, statement II is correct.**
● **Aramaic script:** It was a major **writing system in the Middle East** region in the latter half of the **1st** **millennium BCE**. Derived from the North Semitic script, the Aramaic alphabet was developed in the **10th and 9th centuries BCE**. The Aramaic language and script were used as a lingua franca throughout the Middle East, and documents and inscriptions in the Aramaic alphabet have been found in **Greece,** **India, northern Arabia, and Egypt. So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the history of medieval India,
the Hussain Shahi dynasty and Ilyas Shahi dynasty
ruled over which of the following regions?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Bengal** was ruled over by two powerful dynasties, **Ilyas Shahi** (1342-1481) **and Husain Shahis** (14941538).
● The founder of the Ilyas Shahi dynasty was Ilyas Shah (1342-1357) who was originally a noble of Muhammad Tughlaq and served him at Delhi.
● Alauddin Hussain Shah (1493-1519) got power and laid the foundation of Husain Shahi dynasty. He not only succeeded in subduing Abyssinian slaves but also adopted a rigorous expansionist policy. Under him, the Bengal frontiers reached upto Saran and Bihar. The reign of Husain Shahi dynasty ended in 1538 with the fall of Gaur to Sher Shah Sur. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
In the context of Indian Medieval History, the
terms ‘Turushka’, ‘Tajika’ and ‘Parashika’
referred to:

options.c: Groups based on their regions of origin
options.d: Coins used in the capital of the empire

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Historians who have studied Sanskrit texts and inscriptions dating between the eighth and fourteenth centuries point out that the term **musalmaan or Muslim** was virtually never used. Instead, people were occasionally identified in terms of the region from which they came.
● They included terms such as **Turushka, Tajika, Mlechchha, Parasika, Yavana, Hammira, Gori, Turaka,** **Matanga and Garjanaka**. Only in the Veraval inscription of the time of Vaghela Arjunadeva, issued in the year 1264, one finds the term Musalmaan used to denote the Muslims.
● The **Turkish rulers were designated as Turushka, Tajika were people from Tajikistan and** **Parashika were people from Persia**.
● Sometimes, terms used for other peoples were applied to the new migrants. For instance, the Turks and Afghans were referred to as **Shakas** and **Yavanas** (a term used for Greeks).
● Between fifteenth and seventeenth centuries too, Yavana, Shaka and Turushka were used but new words like **Pathana**, **Mugil**, **Sultana** and **Patrishaha** also made their appearance. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Who were the mlechchhas?**
● A general term for the migrant communities was used as mlechchha, indicating that **they** **did not observe the norms of caste society** and spoke languages that were not derived from Sanskrit.
● Such terms sometimes had a derogatory connotation, but they **rarely denoted a distinct** **religious community** of Muslims in opposition to Hindus.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Sufi Term Meaning
I. - Khanqah Place for Sufi gatherings
II. - Murshid Disciple
III. Murid Spiritual guide
IV. Wali Subsistence allowance
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
In the early centuries of Islam, a group of religious minded people called **sufis** turned to asceticism and mysticism in protest against the growing materialism of the Caliphate as a religious and political institution. They were **critical of the dogmatic definitions and scholastic methods of interpreting the Quran** and sunna (traditions of the Prophet) adopted by theologians. Instead, they **laid emphasis on seeking salvation through intense** **devotion and love for God** by following his commands, and by following the example of the Prophet Muhammad. The sufis thus sought an interpretation of the Quran on the basis of their personal experience.
● By the eleventh century Sufism evolved into a well-developed movement with a body of literature on Quranic studies and sufi practices. Institutionally, the sufis began to organise communities around the **khanqah,** the hermitage of Sufi saints known as shaikh (in Arabic). **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● Shaikhs are called **pir or murshid in Persian. So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● The pir enrolled **disciples (murids)** and appointed a successor (khalifa). **Murids** used to seek spiritual guidance and enlightenment from a **murshid**. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● The term **"wali" denotes a "friend of God"** or a saint, someone who has attained a high spiritual level through devotion and closeness to the divine. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.**
○ **Maddad-i-Mash** was the subsistence allowance given to the scholars during the mughal period. **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following personalities:
I. 	 Shaikh Muinuddin Sijzi
II. 	 Khwaja Qutbuddin Bakhtiyar Kaki
III. 	Shaikh Nizamuddin Auliya
IV. 	 Muhammad Baha’uddin Shah Naqshband
How many of the above were associated with the
Chishti Silsilah?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Chishti Silsilah** is one of the most influential Sufi orders in the Indian subcontinent, originating from the town of **Chisht** in central Afghanistan. The Chishti tradition became prominent in India from the late twelfth century.
● **Major teachers of the Chishti silsilah**:
**Sufi Teacher** **Location of Dargah**
Shaikh Muinuddin Sijzi (Moinuddin Chisti) Ajmer (Rajasthan). **So, point I is correct.**
Khwaja Qutbuddin Bakhtiyar Kaki Delhi. **So, point II is correct.**
Shaikh Nizamuddin Auliya Delhi. **So, point III is correct.**
Shaikh Nasiruddin Chiragh-i Dehli Delhi
● **Muhammad Baha’uddin Shah Naqshband** (1317–1389 CE) was a towering Sufi master and the eponymous founder of the Naqshbandi order, one of the most influential Sufi lineages in the Islamic world. **So, point IV is not correct.** **So, only three of the above personalities were associated with the Chishti Silsilah.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts about Chishti Silsilah:**
● The **khanqah** (hospice) was at the centre of social life for the Chishtis, where people from various walks of life, soldiers, slaves, merchants, poets, travelers, rich and poor, as well as Hindu jogis and qalandars, came seeking discipleship, amulets, healing, and intercession of the Shaikh.
● The Chishti tradition became known for **ziyarat** (pilgrimage) and **qawwali** (devotional music).
● In Delhi, the Chishti Silsilah conversed in **Hindavi**, and other Sufis like Baba Farid used the local language incorporated in the **Guru Granth Sahib**.
● Sufi poetry in the Deccan (Bijapur, Karnataka) in **Dakhani** (a variant of Urdu) included short poems sung by women during household chores like spinning and grinding grain, showing the wide social base of the Chishti order.
● A major feature of the Chishti tradition was its **austerity** and its practice of maintaining a distance from political power, though unsolicited grants from political elites were accepted.
● Sufi saints like Nizamuddin Auliya were sometimes given titles such as **sultan-ul-** **mashaikh** (king among shaikhs) and were asked to perform rituals for royal benefit, though instances of conflict with Sultans also occurred.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to Siddis of Janjira:
I. 	 The Siddi admirals were enrolled as Mughal
Mansabdars.
II. 	 The Portuguese supported the Siddis of
Janjira against Shivaji.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Siddis were **Abyssinians** who had settled at Janjira in the 15th century. The Marathas came into direct contact with the Siddis during their operations in Konkan in pursuit of Afzal Khan (1659). The Siddis, as Bijapur vassals, supported Afzal Khan. Shivaji sent a strong force under Raghunath Ballal to suppress the Siddis.
● Since 1671 **the entire naval force of the Siddis was transferred to the Mughals and the Siddi admirals** (Siddi Qasim and Siddi Khairiyat) were enrolled as Mughal Mansabdars and the Siddi fleet was taken over by the Mughals on the same terms as those of Bijapur. The Siddis later recovered Ganda from the Marathas (1671). Shivaji tried to get English help but failed. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Maratha-Portuguese relations were also far from cordial from the very beginning. ○ **The Portuguese supported the Siddis of Janjira against Shivaji**. They gave refuge to the desais of south Ratnagiri who were dispossessed by Shivaji in Goa. Shivaji also claimed chauth from Daman. The presence of Shivaji's forces on the western coast also hindered Portuguese trade. ○ But in spite of these differences, the Portuguese **avoided a direct clash** with the Marathas and treated them friendly. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Princess Jahanara, daughter of Mughal emperor
Shah Jahan, wrote “Munis al Arwah”, a biography of which one of the following Sufi saints?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Princess Jahanara**, daughter of Mughal emperor Shah Jahan, wrote “Munis al Arwah”, a biography of **Shaikh Muinuddin Chishti**.
● Demonstrating her humility, she refers to herself as a “lowly faqira” and recounts her journey from Agra to Ajmer in her father's company, with a commitment to performing extra prayers at every stop along the way. During the pilgrimage, **Jahanara displayed intense reverence** for the saint.
● Jahanara’s account also highlights that **ziyarat** (pilgrimage to a Sufi tomb) involved rituals of music and dance, with specially trained **qawwals** performing mystical chants to evoke divine ecstasy. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements about Amir
Khusrau:
I. 	 He created a style of Persian poetry known as
Sabaq-i-Hindi.
II. 	 His work Khazain-ul-Futuh provides an
account of the reign of Alauddin Khalji.
III. 	He promoted Hindavi as a language of the
masses.
IV. 	 He created the traditional form of Qawwali.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Amir Khusrau** (1252–1325), known as “Tooti-e-Hind” or the **singing bird of India**, was a prolific writer, poet, musician, linguist, and historian whose works hold immense importance for the cultural history of medieval India.
● In literature, Khusrau wrote in Persian and experimented with various poetic forms, creating a distinctive **Sabaq-i-Hindi** or Indian style. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Khazain-ul-Futuh**, also known as Tarikh-i-Alai, is an account written by Amir Khusrau detailing the **first fifteen years of Sultan Alauddin Khalji’s reign**. In this work, Khusrau describes the Sultan’s major military expeditions to regions such as Gujarat, Chittor, Malwa, and Warangal. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● He promoted **Hindavi** as a language of the masses and incorporated mixed vocabulary in his poetry, laying the groundwork for the development of Urdu. **So, statement III is correct.**
● He is **credited with the creation of Qawwali.** Qawwali is an energetic musical performance of Sufi poetry aimed at leading listeners to a state of religious ecstasy. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Performing Arts (Dance, Drama, Music, Martial Arts)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Music Feature
I. - Kirtan Involves singing and dancing
II. - Abhangas Sung in praise of Vithobha God
III. - Doha Couplets of poetry
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Kirtanas** refer to the community singing for praising God. They originated in West Bengal and involve singing and dancing and take inspiration from the Gita Govinda. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Abhnagas** originated in Maharashtra. They are sung in praise of **Vithobha God**. These songs were composed and sung by bhakti saints Tukaram, Namdev, etc. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Dohas refer to the couplets of poetry.** Kabir's dohas are rhyming couplets that offer spiritual and philosophical wisdom in simple, accessible language, teaching lessons on ethics, perseverance, and a compassionate outlook on life. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** **So, all three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the city of Hampi:
I. 	 It includes Krishna temple complex, Vitthala
temple complex and Lotus Mahal complex.
II. 	 Its temples had a unique feature with wide
Chariot streets flanked by the rows of Pillared Mandapas.
III. 	It is known for its adoption of elements of Indo
Islamic Architecture in secular buildings.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Hampi, located in present-day Karnataka, is one of the most significant historical and archaeological sites in India. It **served as the capital of the Vijayanagara Empire**. The city is now recognized as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. **The name "Hampi" is a derivative of "Pampa," which is another name for the** **Hindu goddess Pampadevi, who was worshipped locally.**
● It houses the **Krishna temple complex**, Narasimha, Ganesa, Hemakuta group of temples, Achyutaraya temple complex, **Vitthala temple complex,** Pattabhirama temple complex, **Lotus Mahal complex**. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● Another unique feature of temples at Hampi is the **wide Chariot streets flanked by the rows of Pillared** **Mandapas**, introduced when chariot festivals became an integral part of the rituals. The stone chariot in front of the temple is also testimony to its religious ritual. Most of the structures at Hampi are constructed from local granite, burnt bricks and lime mortar. **So, statement II is correct.**
● It is also known for its **adoption of elements of Indo Islamic Architecture in secular buildings like the** **Queen’s Bath and the Elephant Stables**, representing a highly evolved multi-religious and multi-ethnic society. Building activity in Hampi continued over a period of 200 years reflecting the evolution in the religious and political scenario as well as the advancements in art and architecture. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Arrange the following dynasties of the Vijayanagara empire in chronological order of their rule:
I. 	 Aravidu
II. 	 Sangama
III. 	Saluva
IV. 	 Tuluvas
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Between **1336 and 1565**, Vijayanagar was ruled by three different dynasties. The **Sangama dynasty** remained in power till **1485**. The greatest ruler of the Sangama dynasty was **Deva Raya II.** After his death, the Sangama dynasty became weak.
● The next dynasty, the **Saluva dynasty** **founded by** **Saluva Narasimha** reigned only for a brief period **(1486-1503).**
● By 1503 the Saluva dynasty was supplanted by the **Tuluva dynasty**. It was founded by **Vira Narasimha.** The greatest of the Vijayanagar rulers, **Krishna Deva Raya** **belonged to the Tuluva dynasty.**
● The last dynasty was the **Aravidu dynasty** which ruled till the seventeenth century. Thirumala, **Sri Ranga** **and Venkata II** were the important rulers of this dynasty. The last ruler of the Vijayanagar kingdom was Sri Ranga III. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
In the Vijayanagara Empire, the term "Kudirai
Chettis" referred to which of the following group?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Kudirai Chettis in the Vijayanagara Empire were local merchant communities who specialized** **in the trade of horses**, a crucial commodity for warfare during that period. Since effective cavalry units were essential for military success, the **import of horses from regions like Arabia and Central Asia** was tightly linked to power struggles among rival kingdoms.
● Initially, this horse trade was dominated by Arab traders, but the Kudirai Chettis actively participated in these exchanges, **facilitating the supply of horses to the empire**. Their role became especially significant as control over horse trade directly influenced the strength of cavalry forces, which were vital in the ongoing conflicts of the time. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to Krishnadeva Raya, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 He belonged to the Suluva dynasty.
II. 	 He added gopurams to many temples in South
India.
III. 	He founded the city of Nagalapuram.
IV. 	 He assumed the title “Establisher of the
Yavana Kingdom”.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Krishnadeva Raya** was the most illustrious ruler of the Vijayanagara Empire. Under his leadership, the empire reached its zenith in political power, military strength, economic prosperity, and cultural brilliance.
● **Krishnadeva Raya belonged to the Tuluva dynasty**, **which came to power after the decline of the** **Suluva dynasty** around 1503. The earliest dynasty, the Sangama, ruled until 1485, followed by the Saluvas who controlled the empire until the Tuluvas, including Krishnadeva Raya, assumed power. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● He is well-known for his significant contributions to temple architecture. **He built several fine temples** **and notably added impressive gopurams (monumental entrance towers) to many important temples** **across South India.** These architectural additions are part of his enduring legacy and reflect his patronage of art and culture during a period marked by prosperity. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Krishnadeva Raya founded the suburban township of Nagalapuram near the Vijayanagara capital,** naming it in honor of his mother. Nagalapuram became an important cultural and administrative center, highlighting his role not only as a warrior-king but also as a builder and founder. **So, statement III is** **correct.**
● **Krishnadeva Raya took on the grand title “Establisher of the Yavana Kingdom”, which symbolized** **his military successes against the Muslim rulers of the Deccan,** often referred to as Yavanas in contemporary records. His reign was characterized by military expansion, including conquests in the Raichur doab and victories over the rulers of Orissa and Bijapur, cementing his dominance in the region. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 In the Battle of Diwani of 1509, Krishnadevaraya defeated the Sultan of Bijapur and annexed the Raichur Doab.
II. 	 In the Battle of Talikota of 1565, the combined
forces of the Deccan Sultanates defeated the Vijayanagara Empire.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Battle of Diwani in 1509 was a significant victory for Krishnadevaraya over the Sultan of** **Bijapur.** During this battle, Krishnadevaraya’s forces successfully defeated the Sultan Mahmud and ended the annual raids and plundering of Vijayanagara towns and villages by the Deccan Sultanates. **The** **victory also resulted in the annexation of the Raichur Doab, a strategic region between the Krishna** **and Tungabhadra rivers. So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Battle of Rakshasi-Tangadi, also known as the Battle of Talikota, took place in 1565 and was a** **decisive and catastrophic defeat for the Vijayanagara Empire**. The combined armies of the Deccan Sultanates - Bijapur, Ahmadnagar, and Golconda united against Vijayanagara forces led by **Rama Raya**, the empire’s chief minister. The battle resulted in a crushing defeat, the sacking of Vijayanagara city, and the eventual decline and abandonment of the empire’s capital. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Amara-Nayaka system of the Vijayanagara empire:
I. 	 It was influenced by the Iqta system of the
Delhi Sultanate.
II. 	 Amara-Nayakas were permanently posted in
the provinces.
III. 	Amara-Nayakas were entitled to collect
revenue from their assigned territories.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Amara-Nayaka system** was a key administrative and military institution of the **Vijayanagara Empire**, functioning between the 14th and 17th centuries CE. It was instrumental in maintaining control over vast territories and mobilizing military support.
● The **Amara-Nayaka system shared similarities with the Iqta** **system of Delhi Sultanate**, where military commanders (Iqta holders or Amara-Nayakas) were granted control over territories in exchange for military service and tribute. Both systems functioned as a means to delegate administrative authority and reduce the burden of direct governance. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● The Amara-Nayakas were **not permanently posted**. In fact, **Vijayanagara kings retained control by** **transferring nayakas** from one region to another to prevent the accumulation of independent regional power. This allowed the central authority (Raya) to check rebellious tendencies and maintain administrative flexibility. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Amara-Nayakas collected revenue from peasants, artisans, and traders in their allotted regions. They kept a portion for themselves and used the rest to **maintain a fixed number of troops, including horses and** **elephants**, which were essential for the empire’s military campaigns. Additionally, part of the revenue was used for **temple maintenance and irrigation infrastructure**. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Foreign Traveller
Observation about the Vijayanagara Empire
I. - Domingo Paes
Compared the city of Vijayanagara with Rome
II. - Abdur Razzaq
Seven concentric lines of forts to protect the city
III. - Duarte Barbosa
Housing based on occupational groups
IV. - Nicolo Conti
Slavery was a common practice
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Foreign travellers** visiting the Vijayanagara Empire between the 14th and 16th centuries left **rich and** **vivid accounts of the empire's urban layout, architecture, economic activity, and social life**. These observations provide invaluable insights into the functioning and grandeur of the empire.
● **Domingo Paes**, a **Portuguese** traveller who visited during the reign of **Krishnadeva Raya**, was deeply impressed by the scale and grandeur of Vijayanagara city. He compared it to **Rome**, stating that it was **"****as large as Rome, and very beautiful to the sight****,"** highlighting the city’s expansive layout, groves, lakes, and water conduits.  **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Abdur Razzaq**, an ambassador from **Persia**, visited in the 15th century and was struck by the **massive** **fortifications** of the city. He described **seven concentric lines of forts**, which protected not just the city but also agricultural lands and forests. His detailed description of bastions, wedge-shaped stones, and enclosed fields offers a rare glimpse into medieval Indian military architecture. **So, pair II is correctly** **matched.**
**Points To Remember**
● Amara is believed to be derived from the Sanskrit word samara, meaning battle or war.
● Amara also resembles the Persian term amir, meaning a high noble.
● **Duarte Barbosa,** a **Portuguese** traveller, observed that common people's houses in the Vijayanagara empire were "well built and **arranged according to occupations"** and lay along long streets with open spaces. This suggests a planned urban layout, likely supporting guild-based residential clustering. **So, pair** **III is correctly matched.**
● **Nicolo Conti**, an **Italian** traveller who visited Vijayanagara in the early 15th century, **noted the presence** **of slavery** in the region. His account reflects social realities of the time, where bonded labour and servitude were prevalent practices in many societies, including Vijayanagara. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, all four of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Kamalapuram Tank
II. 	 Hiriya Canal
III. 	Anegundi Canal
How many of the above were built during the Vijayanagara period?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Vijayanagara Empire**, established in the 14th century is well known for its **advanced urban** **planning and water management systems**. Given the arid terrain surrounding the city of Hampi, rulers invested heavily in constructing tanks, canals, and aqueducts to conserve and distribute water for agriculture, city use, and religious purposes.
● **Kamalapuram Tank** was one of the earliest and most **significant waterworks of Vijayanagara.** Built in the early 15th century, it not only irrigated nearby fields but also supplied water via channels to the “royal centre.” It exemplifies the empire's sophisticated rainwater harvesting techniques**. So, point I is** **correct.**
● Constructed by rulers of the **Sangama dynasty**, the **Hiriya Canal** drew water from a dam across the Tungabhadra River and irrigated the fertile valley between the sacred centre and urban core. It is a prominent surviving example of Vijayanagara’s hydraulic engineering. **So, point II is correct.**
● The **Anegundi Canal** was constructed during the **Vijayanagara period**. Originating from the Anegundi anicut on the Tungabhadra, it extended for several kilometres and irrigated villages north of Hampi. Even today, traces of this canal are found in functioning water systems in the region, testifying to the longlasting impact of Vijayanagara’s water engineering. **So, point III is correct.** **So, all the three of the above waterworks were built during the Vijayanagara period.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following structures is popularly
known as "A House of Victory"?

options.a: Mahanavami Dibba of the Vijayanagara Empire
options.d: Tomb of Sher Shah Suri at Sasaram

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Mahanavami Dibba** is a grand ceremonial platform located in the Vijayanagara Empire’s capital, **Hampi**. It is popularly known as the **“House of Victory”** because it was the site where the emperor celebrated military victories and held grand Durbar ceremonies during the Mahanavami festival. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **The Rang Mahal in Bidar Fort** is a beautifully decorated palace known for its vibrant interior with intricate tile work and plaster designs. It served as a royal residence and pleasure pavilion. It is celebrated for its artistic excellence and was part of the Bahmani Sultanate’s architectural heritage.
● The **Diwan-i-Khas** in Delhi’s Red Fort was the hall of private audience where Mughal emperors met courtiers and state guests. Known for its exquisite marble work and the
famous Peacock Throne, the Diwan-i-Khas symbolized imperial power.” It was primarily a place for political and diplomatic affairs.
● The **Tomb of Sher Shah Suri** is situated at Sasaram in Bihar. It is an impressive example of Indo-Islamic architecture. The tomb is a symbol of Sher Shah’s legacy and architectural patronage.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which of the following books were authored by
Krishnadevaraya?
I. 	 Amuktamalyada
II. 	 Maduravijayam
III. 	Manucharitam
IV. 	 Jambavati Kalyanam
V. 	 Ushaparinayam
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

options.c: II, III, IV and V only
options.d: I, II, III, IV and V

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Krishnadevaraya** is often hailed as **Andhra Bhoja**. Among the legendary Ashtadiggajas,  the eight great poets of his court, Krishnadevaraya himself was a prolific writer and a literary patron.
● **Amuktamalyada is a Telugu epic poem authored by emperor Krishnadevaraya of the Vijayanagara** **empire**. The work narrates the story of the goddess Andal, a devotee of Lord Vishnu, and beautifully blends devotion with poetic grandeur. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Maduravijayam is a historical Sanskrit text authored by Gangadevi, the wife of the Vijayanagara** **ruler Kampana.** This work primarily chronicles Kampana’s military campaign against the Madurai Sultanate and details the Vijayanagara Empire’s expansion into the Madurai region. **So, point II is not** **correct.**
● **Allasani Peddana,** a prominent poet in the court of Krishnadevaraya and one of the famed Ashtadiggajas, **authored notable works such as Manucharitam and Harikathasaram. So, point III is not correct.**
○ Allasani Peddanna was the greatest and he was called **Andhrakavita Pitamaga** (the grandfather of Telugu poetry).
● **Jambavati Kalyanam is a Sanskrit poetic work authored by Krishnadevaraya.** This literary piece narrates the divine marriage of Lord Krishna to Jambavati, one of his consorts, and is celebrated for its classical style and devotional content. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **Ushaparinayam is a classical Telugu poem composed by Krishnadevaraya.** The work poetically describes the romantic episode of the moon (Usha) and her love for Aniruddha, the grandson of Lord Krishna. **So, point V is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
In the context of Deccan sultanates, which one of
the following is **not** correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Ahmadnagar Sultanate was established in 1490 by Malik Ahmed Nizam Shah I,** who was originally a Bahmani governor before declaring independence. The ruling dynasty was called the **Nizam** **Shahi dynasty.** It was one of the prominent Deccan Sultanates until it fell to the Mughals in 1636. **So,** **option (a) is correct.**
● **The Bijapur Sultanate was founded by Yusuf Adil Shah in 1490**, a former governor under the Bahmani Sultanate who declared independence to form the **Adil Shahi dynasty.** Bijapur was a major power in the Deccan, known for its impressive Indo-Islamic architecture, including the Gol Gumbaz. It played a critical role in the defeat of Vijayanagar at the Battle of Talikota and was eventually annexed by the Mughal Empire in 1686. **So, option (b) is correct.**
● The **Bidar Sultanate was founded by Qasim Barid I**, who established the **Barid Shahi dynasty in 1528**. ○ The **Berar Sultanate was founded by Fathullah Imad-ul-Mulk in 1490**, marking the beginning of the **Imad Shahi dynasty.** Originally a Bahmani governor, he declared independence and established Berar with Achalpur as its capital. Ahmadnagar annexed it in 1574. **So, option (c) is** **not correct.**
● The **Golconda Sultanate was established by Sultan Quli Qutb-ul-Mulk**, a Persian who migrated to the Deccan and served under the Bahmani Sultanate before founding the **Qutb Shahi dynasty** in 1518. Golconda was renowned for its fortified city and iconic monuments such as the Charminar and the Qutb Shahi tombs. It flourished culturally and politically until its fall to the Mughals in 1687 under Aurangzeb’s conquest. **So, option (d) is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Bahmani Kingdom:
I. 	 It was founded by Harihara and Bukka.
II. 	 It was established as an independent state
from the Delhi Sultanate.
III. 	Bidar and Gulburga served as its capitals
during different periods.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Bahmani Sultanate was founded by Alauddin Hasan, who declared independence in 1345 at** **Devagiri** and later shifted his capital to Gulbarga in northern Karnataka. He took the title Bahman Shah, and the dynasty he established ruled from 1347 to 1527. On the other hand, Harihara and Bukka were the founders of the Vijayanagar Kingdom, established earlier in 1336 at Vijayanagara. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
○ From the first half of the 12th century, the conflict between the officers in Deccan and Delhi sultanate accelerated and rebellious armies emerged victorious. This led to the **establishment of** **an independent Bahmani state in the Deccan in 1347 CE** with the capital at Gulbarga in Andhra Pradesh. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The founder of the Bahmani kingdom was **Alauddin Bahman Shah** also known as Hasan Gangu in 1347. Its capital was **Gulbarga**. There were a total of fourteen Sultans ruling over this kingdom. Among them, Alauddin Bahman Shah, Muhammad Shah I and Firoz Shah were important. **Ahmad Wali Shah shifted** **the capital from Gulbarga to Bidar.** The power of the Bahmani kingdom reached its peak under the rule of Muhammad Shah III.  **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **The Bahmani kingdom reached its peak under the guidance of Mahmud Gawan.** He was a Persian merchant. He came to India at the age of forty two and joined the services of the Bahmani kingdom. Slowly he became the chief minister due to his personal qualities. He **was granted the title** **Malik-ut-Tujjar** **(Chief of the Merchants)** **by Sultan Humayun Shah.** Later, he rose to become the wazir (Prime Minister) and was also known as Khwaju-i-Jahan. Mahmud Gawan was a key administrator and strategist who helped the Bahmani kingdom reach its peak during his tenure**.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
In the context of medieval India, which of the
following statements are correct?
I. 	 Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti and Babur were
contemporaries.
II. 	 Saint Vidyaranya and Harihara were contemporaries.
III. 	Saint Tukaram and Shivaji were contemporaries.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● After Muizuddin Muhammad bin Sam of Ghor had defeated Prithviraj Chauhan in the Second Battle of Tarain (1192) and established his rule in Delhi, **Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti** (Born 1143- Died 1236) who came from Iran started living and preaching in Ajmer. He was the most famous of the Sufi saints of India. He had a number of disciples who are called Sufis of the Chishti order. **Babur was invited by** **Daulat Khan Lodi, a rebel of the Lodi dynasty, in 1524,** to invade North India and fight the dynasty. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Vijayanagar was founded in 1336 by **Harihara and Bukka of the Sangama dynasty**. They were originally served under the Kakatiya rulers of Warangal. Then they went to Kampili where they were imprisoned and converted to Islam. **Later, they returned to the Hindu fold at the initiative of the saint** **Vidyaranya.** They also proclaimed their independence and founded a new city on the south bank of the Tungabhadra river. It was called Vijayanagar meaning city of victory. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Tukaram was a Bhakti saint of Maharashtra. He was the contemporary of Shivaji.** He was responsible for creating a background for Maratha nationalism. He opposed all social distinctions. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Treaty of Purandar (1665):
I. 	 It was signed between Shivaji and Raja Jai
Singh of Amber.
II. 	 The Mughals recognised the right of Shivaji
to hold parts of the Bijapur kingdom.
III. 	Shivaji agreed to Mughal authority and
sent his minor son Sambhaji to serve as a mansabdar.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Shivaji opposed the Mughal dynasty and founded the Maratha kingdom in 17th-century India. His kingdom’s security was based on religious toleration and on the functional integration of Brahmans, Marathas, and Prabhus. Shivaji’s military conquests made him a legendary figure in the Maratha region. Many came forward to join his army.
● In 1664, Shivaji attacked **Surat**, the chief port of the Mughals and plundered it. Aurangzeb sent **Raja Jai** **Singh of Amber** to fight against Shivaji. He made elaborate preparations and succeeded in besieging the Purander fort where Shivaji lodged his family and treasure.
● Shivaji opened negotiations with **Jai Singh** and the **Treaty of Purander** was signed in **1665**. Hence, the treaty **was signed between Shivaji and Raja Jai Singh of Amber**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● According to the **Treaty of Purandar,** Shivaji had to **surrender 23 forts to the Mughals out of 35 forts** held by him. The **remaining 12 forts** were to be left to Shivaji on condition of service and loyalty to the Mughal empire. On the other hand, the **Mughals recognized the right of Shivaji** to hold certain parts of the Bijapur kingdom. **So, statement II is correct.**
● As part of the treaty, Shivaji agreed to Mughal authority and sent his minor son **Sambhaji to serve as a** **mansabdar** in the Mughal court. Aurangzeb called Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj and his son Sambhaji to his court in Agra in **1666**. During the proceedings, Chhatrapati Shivaji was dishonored and subsequently placed under house arrest.
● However in **1666,** Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj escaped with his son Sambhaji. Following Chhatrapati Shivaji’s departure from Agra, tensions with the Mughals eased. **Jaswant Singh** served as a **mediator** for peace between Chhatrapati **Shivaji** and **Aurangzeb. Aurangzeb** bestowed the title of **raja upon** **Chhatrapati Shivaji** and reinstated **Sambhaji as a Mughal mansabdar** between 1666 and 1668. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Minister Function
I. - Shurunavis Managing foreign affairs
II. - Sumant Preparing royal orders
III. Amatya Managing the empire’s accounts
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **administrative system of the Marathas** was very much influenced by the administrative system of the **Mughals and the Deccani states**. **Shivaji** laid the foundations of a sound system of administration.
● The king was at the helm of the affairs. He was assisted by a council of ministers called **Ashtapradhan** **mandal**. However, each minister was directly responsible to Shivaji. The Peshwa was in charge of finance and **general administration**. Later he became the prime minister.
● **Responsibilities of the ministers in the Ashtapradhan mandal during the reign of Shivaji:** ○ **Peshwa or Pantpradhan**: Prime Minister responsible for overseeing the entire administration.
○ **Amatya or Mazumdar**: Finance Minister, managing the Empire s accounts. **So, pair III is** **correctly matched.** ○ **Shurunavis or Sacheev**: Secretary, who prepared royal orders. **So, pair I is not correctly** **matched.** ○ **Mantri**: Interior Minister, overseeing internal affairs and intelligence. ○ **Sari-i-Naubat or Senapati:** Commander-in-Chief, in charge of the military and defense. ○ **Sumant or Dabir**: Foreign Minister, managing relationships with other rulers. **So, pair II is not** **correctly matched.** ○ **Nyay Adhyaksh**: Chief Justice, handling civil and criminal justice. ○ **Panditrao**: High Priest, managing religious matters. ○ **Chitnis**: Personal Secretary to the Chhatrapati, acting as a senior writer and sometimes seen as second to the Peshwa in their absence. **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Chauth and Sardeshmukhi taxes levied by
the Marathas:
I. 	 Chauth was an annual tax amounting
to one-fourth of the total revenue while
Sardeshmukhi was levied in addition to the
Chauth.
II. 	 Both were collected only from provinces
under the Maratha kingdom.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Chauth and Sardeshmukhi** were levied by the **Marathas** in the early 18th century. ○ **Chauth** was an **annual tax** amounting to **one-fourth** of the total revenue while **Sardeshmukhi**, collected by **Shivaji** at a rate of **one-tenth** of the total revenue, was a levy assessed **in addition to** **the Chauth. So, statement I is correct.** ○ Together, these taxes provided the Marathas with a **reliable and substantial source of income**. This financial support enabled them to expand their influence beyond Shivaji’s realm, strengthening their position in the region.
● The Marathas often levied those taxes from **provinces that were not directly under them**, including places in the neighbouring territories of the Mughal empire and  **Deccan sultanates.** In return, the Marathas protected those provinces and did not intervene in their internal administration. **So, statement** **II is correct.**
○ Chauth and sardeshmukhi were the taxes collected not in the Maratha kingdom but in the neighbouring territories of the Mughal empire or Deccan sultanates. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the military conquests of Babur:
I. 	 He introduced a new war tactic tulughma in
the first Battle of Panipat, 1526.
III. 	He assumed the title of Ghazi after the Battle
of Khanua, 1527.
III. 	He proclaimed himself as “Emperor of
Hindustan” after the defeat of Medini Rai in
the Battle of Chanderi, 1528.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **First Battle of Panipat** was fought between the invading forces of Babur and the Lodi Empire, which took place on 21 April 1526 in North India. It marked the beginning of the Mughal Empire. The Mughal forces of Babur defeated the much larger ruling army of **Ibrahim Lodi.**
● Babur’s success in the battle was due his **cavalry and artillery**. Babur occupied Delhi and sent his son Humayun to seize Agra. Babur proclaimed himself as **“Emperor of Hindustan”.** The new war tactics introduced by Babur were the **tulughma and the araba**. **So, statement I is correct and statement III is** **not correct.**
○ **Tulughma** meant dividing the whole army into various units, viz. the Left, the Right and the Centre. The Left and Right divisions were further subdivided into Forward and Rear divisions. ○ Through this a small army could be used to surround the enemy from all the sides. The Centre Forward division was then provided with **carts (araba)** which were placed in rows facing the enemy and tied to each other with animal hide ropes. ○ **Medini Rai** was defeated in the **Battle of Chanderi (1528).**
● Babur s subsequent **victories over Rana Sangha and the Afghans** secured his position as the ruler of India. Rana Sangha of Mewar was a great Rajput warrior. He marched against Babur in the **Battle of** **Khanua** (near Agra) in **1527.** Babur won a decisive victory over Rana Sangha of Mewar and **Babur** **assumed the title, Ghazi**. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following traditions of succession
was followed in the Mughal dynasty?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Mughals **did not believe in the rule of primogeniture**, where the eldest son inherited his father’s estate. Instead, they followed the Mughal and Timurid custom of **coparcenary inheritance**, or a **division** **of the inheritance amongst all the sons.**
● The Mughals were **descendants of two great lineages of rulers.** From their **mother’s side** they were descendants of **Genghis Khan** (died 1227), the Mongol ruler who ruled over parts of China and Central Asia. From their **father’s side**, they were the successors of **Timur** (died 1404), the ruler of Iran, Iraq and modern-day Turkey.
● However, the Mughals did not like to be called Mughal or Mongol. This was because Genghis Khan’s memory was associated with the massacre of innumerable people. It was also linked with the Uzbegs, their Mongol competitors. On the other hand, the Mughals were **proud of their Timurid ancestry,** not least of all because their great ancestor had captured Delhi in 1398. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the revenue administration in the Mughal empire:
I. 	 While watan jagirs were transferable every
three or four years, tankha jagirs remained hereditary and non-transferable.
II. 	 Mansabdars had revenue collection rights for
jagir assignments.
III. 	The zabti system as devised by Todar Mal
was based on fixed rates for each crop.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Mansabdari system was a grading system used by the Mughals to **fix rank, salary and military** **responsibilities.** Rank and salary were determined by **zat**, wherein the higher a zat, the more prestigious was the noble’s position in court and the larger his salary.
○ The **Jagirdari system was an integral part of the mansabdari system** which developed under **Akbar** and underwent certain changes during the reign of his successors. During Akbar’s period all the territory was broadly divided into two: **Khalisa** (Crown Land) and **Jagir** (Land Assignments).
● There were generally **four types** of revenue assignments (Jagirs): ○ Jagirs which were assigned to **zamindars (chieftains)** in their homelands, were called **watan** **jagirs.** Under Jahangir some Muslim nobles were given jagirs resembling watan jagir called **al-** **tamgha**. ○ Jagirs, which were given in lieu of pay, were known as jagir **tankha**; ○ Jagirs given to a person on certain conditions were called **mashrut** jagirs; ○ Jagirs which involved no obligation of service and were independent of rank were called **in’am** **jagirs;** and ○ **Tankha jagirs** were transferable every three or four years, **watan jagirs remained hereditary** **and non-transferable.** Sometimes watan jagir was converted into khalisa for a certain period as Aurangzeb did in case of Jodhpur in 1679.  **So, statement I is not correct.**
● But unlike muqtis, most mansabdars **did not actually reside** **in or administer** **their jagirs.** They only had rights to the revenue of their assignments, which was **collected for them by their servants** while the mansabdars themselves served in some other part of the country. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Zabti** was the most important method of assessment. The origin of this practice is traced to **Sher** **Shah**. During **Akbar’s reign**, the system was revised a number of times before it took the final shape. Akbar’s revenue minister, **Todar Mal**, carried out a careful survey of crop yields, prices and areas
cultivated for **a 10-year period**, 1570–1580. On the basis of this data, tax was fixed on **each crop in cash**. **So, statement III is correct.**
● Each province was divided into **revenue circles** with its own schedule of revenue rates for individual crops. This revenue system was known as **zabt**.
● The **main features of the zabti system** as it finally came into operation under Akbar were: ○ Measurement of land was essential; ○ **Fixed cash revenue rates** known as **dastur-ul amal or dastur** for each crop. ○ All the collection was made in **cash (and not kind)**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **What was Sher Shah’s system of revenue administration that was later modified by** **Akbar?**
● Sher Shah had established a **rai or per bigha** yield for lands which were under continuous cultivation **(polaj),** or those land which very rarely allowed to lie fallow **(parauti).**
● The rai was based on three rates, representing good, middling and low yields and one third of the sum of these was appropriated as land revenue.
● Akbar adopted **Sher Shah’s rai.**
● Akbar introduced his so-called **karori** **experiment** and appointed **karoris** all over North India in 1574- 75. The entire jagir was converted into **khalisa**.
● On the basis of the information provided by the karoris regarding the actual produce, local prices, productivity, etc. in 1580, Akbar instituted a new system **ain dahsala**, where the average produce, of different crops as well as the average prices prevailing over the last ten years (15-24 R.Y. of Akbar) were calculated.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
"As in the wide expanse of the divine compassion
there is room for all classes and the followers of all creeds, so … in his Imperial dominions, which on all sides were limited only by the sea, there was room for the professors of opposite religions, and for beliefs, good and bad, and the road to intolerance was closed." The above statement was made in reference to
which one of the following?

options.c: Raja Raja Chola I’s policy of temple building

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Jahangir,** Akbar’s son, described his father **Akbar’s policy of** **sulh-i kul** in the following words: ○ “As in the wide expanse of the divine compassion there is room for all classes and the followers of all creeds, so … in his Imperial dominions, which on all sides were limited only by the sea, there was room for the professors of opposite religions, and for beliefs, good and bad, and the road to intolerance was closed. Sunnis and Shias met in one mosque and Christians and Jews in one church to pray. He consistently followed the principle of “universal peace” (sulh-i kul)”
● The agent of God **could not practice discrimination among the various faiths** present in society. A doctrine for justifying the tolerant religious policy was now the need of the hour. **Sovereignty was not** **restricted to any particular faith**. It became overarching.
● They believed **all religions were, in essence, the same but only the paths varied**. Abul Fazl believed that in a polyreligious country like India, the theory of monarchical sovereignty was more relevant. Here, sovereignty was not to be related with any particular religion as the monarch was above all the religions.
● He **promoted the good values of different religions** and thus, assembled different faiths for maintaining peace everywhere. He had to sustain those qualities by adopting an appropriate religious status. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **What was Din e-Ilahi?**
● Akbar wanted a wider concept of religion. He sought for a new justification of religious thoughts and Abul Fazal provided this to him. Abul Fazal told him the new meaning of sovereignty as a divine light. Later on, Akbar portrayed himself as an agent of god who worked on his behalf.
● In the new approach, Fazal elevated allegiance to Akbar **above the constraints of** **any single religious community**, and in effect creating a cult of loyalty around the personality of the emperor. The cult of tawḥīd-e elāhī or dīn-e elāhī seems to have been modeled primarily on the **Sufi pīr-morīd relationship** in which the disciple pledges absolute obedience and devotion to his master. It was Abu’lFażl’s intention to endow the office of emperor with an ideology that would **replace the Sunni** **šarīʿa** as the sole principle by which the state was run.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the historical works of Mughal Empire:
I. 	 Ain-i Akbari, a part of Akbar Nama, is a
compilation of the system of administration written by Abul Fazl.
II. 	 Tuzuk-i-Baburi, the memoir of Babur, was
originally written in Chaghatai Turkish language.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Abul Fazal’s most famous and definitive works, the **Akbar Nama and Ain-i-Akbari**, were composed in the latter part of the sixteenth century. It marks a decisive and **schematic departure from the** **predominant historiographic format of the time**, as it does in several other aspects of the construction of an alternative world view. **Akbar Nama and Ain-i-Akbari together constitute a single book**. Ain – e –Akbari is the third **part of Akbar Nama**. The work was undertaken in 1595 and, after five revisions, completed in 1602. Akbar Nama is divided into three parts as follows:
○ The first part of the Akbar Nama- contains an account of Akbar’s ancestors, including that of his father Humayun. ○ The second part - gives the most complete account of Akbar’s reign up to the 46th year, in a chronological order. ○ The Third part of Akbar Nama is **Ain-i-Akbari- which is a unique compilation of the system of** **administration** and control over the various departments of government in a great empire. It faithfully and minutely records, to the minutest detail, a wide array of facts illustrating its extent, resources, condition, population, industry and wealth as the abundant material supplied from official sources could furnish.
